```markdown
# The forest beneath the forest

Walk through a forest after rain and it is tempting to think the important action is above you: leaves catching light, trunks widening year by year, birds moving between branches. But much of the forest’s metabolism is below the path. Under the litter layer, roots are threaded through with fungal filaments so fine that a teaspoon of soil can hold an intricate living mesh. These fungi are not decoration on tree roots. They are part of how forests feed, recover, compete and store carbon.

The relationship is called mycorrhiza, from the Greek for “fungus-root”. In a mycorrhizal association, a fungus colonises a plant’s root system and extends outward into the soil as microscopic filaments called hyphae. The plant supplies carbon-rich compounds made through photosynthesis. The fungus, in return, helps the plant acquire nutrients, especially phosphorus and nitrogen, and often water and trace elements from beyond the reach of the roots themselves. Mycorrhizal fungi form nutritional symbioses with more than 80% of plant species, and their underground networks can represent a substantial share of living microbial biomass in soils.

For a tree, this is not a minor upgrade. Roots are thick, expensive structures compared with fungal hyphae. A root hair may explore the soil immediately around it; a fungal filament can slip into smaller pores, cross tiny gaps, and spread through patches of decomposing leaf litter or mineral soil. The fungus effectively gives the tree a larger, finer and more chemically versatile absorbing surface. Some ectomycorrhizal fungi, common on many pines, birches, oaks, beeches and eucalypts, form a sheath around root tips and grow between root cells. Arbuscular mycorrhizal fungi, found across a very large range of plants, enter root cells and form tiny branched structures where exchange occurs. The architecture differs, but the underlying bargain is similar: carbon flows from plant to fungus; soil-derived resources flow from fungus to plant.

The bargain is also dynamic. Trees do not simply hand over sugar to any fungus in the neighbourhood. Fungi vary in what they can provide, where they forage, and how much carbon they demand. Some are better at scavenging inorganic phosphorus; others can help mobilise nitrogen from organic matter. Recent reviews of ectomycorrhizal forests emphasise that nitrogen transfer is highly variable, depending on fungal identity, soil conditions and the form in which nitrogen is held.   The result is less like a pipe and more like a shifting set of trade relationships. A tree growing in poor soil may invest heavily in fungal partners; a tree in fertilised or nitrogen-rich conditions may invest differently because the return on that carbon has changed.

This is where the popular phrase “wood-wide web” enters the story, and where accuracy matters. A common mycorrhizal network is not just “fungi in the soil”. It means a physical fungal connection linking the roots of two or more plants. Such connections can, in principle, move carbon, nutrients, water or chemical signals between plants. But proving that this happens in intact forests is difficult. Researchers have to separate transfer through fungal hyphae from other routes: roots leaking compounds into soil, microbes consuming and re-releasing them, water moving dissolved chemicals, or plants responding to airborne signals. A 2023 review in Nature Ecology & Evolution argued that several strong public claims about common mycorrhizal networks in forests, including that they are widespread, that they reliably improve seedling performance, and that mature trees preferentially send resources to offspring, are not yet supported by enough field evidence.

That does not make the networks imaginary. It means they are harder to demonstrate than the metaphor suggests. One of the classic experiments was published in 1997 by Suzanne Simard and colleagues. Working with paper birch and Douglas-fir seedlings in British Columbia, the researchers used carbon isotopes to trace movement between trees associated with ectomycorrhizal fungi. They found bidirectional carbon transfer between the two species, with a net gain by Douglas-fir under the conditions studied.   The experiment became famous because it suggested that forest plants were not merely competing as isolated individuals. Their roots and fungi could be part of a shared below-ground system.

The more careful conclusion is narrower and more interesting. Carbon can move between plants in some experimental settings. Fungal pathways may contribute. But a detected transfer is not the same as proof that a mature tree is deliberately feeding a seedling, or that the transfer meaningfully changes survival in all forests. Later reviews have found mixed evidence on whether access to common mycorrhizal networks improves, harms or has no effect on seedlings. In some cases, being connected may give a seedling access to nutrients or water. In others, the seedling may lose carbon to fungi, face stronger below-ground competition, or gain no measurable advantage. Forest life is not a morality play about generosity. It is an ecological economy with uneven terms.

The “communication” claim needs the same discipline. Plants certainly detect and respond to chemical cues. When leaves are attacked, they can alter defensive chemistry; when neighbours are damaged, nearby plants may respond. Some experiments suggest that mycorrhizal networks can mediate stress-related signalling. In one study, damaged Douglas-fir was reported to transfer carbon and defence-related signals to neighbouring ponderosa pine through ectomycorrhizal networks.   But this is not conversation in the human sense. There is no evidence of intention, language or conscious warning. A more accurate analogy is a biochemical alarm system: changes in one organism can alter the chemical environment of connected organisms, which may then shift their own physiology.

Even the fungal partner should not be treated as a passive cable. The fungus is alive, metabolically active and under selection to acquire carbon and reproduce. It may allocate nutrients towards hosts that supply more carbon, proliferate in nutrient-rich patches, or reduce exchange when conditions change. Some mycorrhizal relationships look mutualistic; others drift towards parasitism when one partner receives less than it gives. The same fungus that helps one tree may compete with another for nitrogen. The same network that appears to connect plants may also allow fungi to manage their own resources across multiple hosts.

The physical structure of these networks changes how soil works. Hyphae bind particles into aggregates, move nutrients across small distances, and leave behind fungal residues when they die. Those residues can become part of longer-lived soil organic matter, especially when microbial remains bind to minerals. But mycorrhizal fungi do not simply “store carbon” in a straightforward way. They receive plant carbon, build tissue, respire carbon dioxide, interact with decomposer microbes and, in some cases, stimulate decomposition as they mine organic matter for nutrients. Recent forest carbon research has found strong links between fungal community composition and soil carbon patterns, but it also shows why simple claims are risky: different fungi can push carbon cycling in different directions.

A concrete example is a conifer forest growing on nutrient-poor soil. The trees may allocate a large share of photosynthetic carbon below ground because nutrients, not light, are the limiting constraint. Ectomycorrhizal fungi form sheaths around root tips, extend hyphae into the soil, and use enzymes and organic acids to access forms of nitrogen or phosphorus the tree cannot easily reach alone. The visible mushrooms that appear after rain are only the fruiting bodies. Most of the organism is the mycelium threaded through roots and soil. When forestry machinery compacts soil, when topsoil is removed, or when a clear-fell changes temperature and moisture, the living conditions for this mycelium can change abruptly.

Another example is forest regeneration after disturbance. Seedlings do not enter a neutral underground world. They germinate into soil already occupied by fungi, roots, bacteria and decaying organic matter. In some settings, existing mycorrhizal fungi can help young trees establish by rapidly colonising their roots. In others, the existing fungal community may favour some tree species over others, shaping which seedlings survive. This is one reason forest recovery is not just a matter of planting stems in the ground. The below-ground community can influence whether those stems become a functioning forest.

The story also matters for climate and conservation. Mycorrhizal fungi are involved in plant productivity, nutrient cycling and soil carbon dynamics at large scales. A 2025 Nature study mapping global mycorrhizal fungal richness reported that mycorrhizal fungi form extensive underground networks and that many biodiversity hotspots for these fungi fall outside protected areas.   That does not mean protecting fungi is a simple climate solution. It means forest policy that ignores below-ground biodiversity is missing part of the system it is trying to manage.

The best way to understand mycorrhizal networks is to abandon both extremes. They are not magical forest internet cables through which wise old trees send care packages to their children. Nor are they a debunked fantasy. Mycorrhizal fungi are real, ancient and ecologically consequential. They help trees acquire nutrients, alter competition, influence seedling establishment, build and break down soil carbon, and sometimes connect plants in ways that allow materials or signals to move between them.

The forest beneath the forest is therefore not a hidden utopia. It is infrastructure, marketplace, digestive system and battleground at once. Its exchanges are chemical rather than sentimental; its networks are patchy, changing and difficult to measure. That makes them less tidy than the “wood-wide web” metaphor, but more impressive. A forest is not just a collection of trees. It is a layered community of organisms negotiating matter and energy above and below ground, with fungi doing much of the work where light cannot reach.

  the prose at a chosen intensity, or composes new prose from a brief while
  avoiding AI patterns. Use this skill for content writing, editing and review. Triggers on

# Human-eyes

The skill helps writers see what marks their prose as machine-flavoured by surfacing patterns and explaining them. Removing or replacing them is opt-in.

Three principles cut across the skill:

1. **The writer decides what to do.** The audit shows every flagged pattern with its location and a short explanation. There is no filter and no ranking. The writer reads the audit and chooses whether to act, and how.
2. **The writer chooses the depth when the skill generates new prose.** Audit and Suggestions return everything regardless of depth. Rewrite and Write run at a depth the writer picks (Balanced or All).
3. **Every interaction with text starts with an audit.** "Human-eyes this" reads as Audit alone, not Rewrite. Direct rewrite requests still audit first; the findings are part of the Rewrite output. The audit teaches the writer to spot the pattern; whatever happens after is the writer's call.

The skill does not score the draft or classify authorship. It does not modify the writer's source file. After an audit, it asks what the writer wants next.

---

## Action selection

Four actions:

| Action | When to fire | Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Audit** *(default)* | The writer hands the agent a draft and asks the skill to look at it, or hands over a draft with no action specified. Triggers: "audit this", "check this for AI", "does this sound AI", "human-eyes this", "what's flagged". | Every flagged pattern with its location in the prose and a short explanation. |
| **Suggestions** | The writer asks for replacements after seeing an audit. Triggers: "give me suggestions", "what should I change", "show me alternatives", "list the fixes". | One suggestion per flagged tell. |
| **Rewrite** | The writer asks for a redrafted version, or confirms a rewrite after an audit. Triggers: "rewrite this", "fix it", "clean it up", "strip the AI tells", "audit and rewrite at <depth>". | An audit followed by a rewrite at the chosen depth. |
| **Write** | The writer hands the agent a brief and no draft. Triggers: "write a 500-word post on X", "draft an opening paragraph about Y", "compose a blog post on Z without sounding AI". | A new draft at the chosen depth. |

If the writer's intent is genuinely ambiguous and the agent can ask, ask whether the writer wants an audit or a rewrite, or whether they're handing over a brief for a new draft. If the agent can't ask (headless invocation, no human turn), default to Audit only.

---

## Action 1: Audit (default)

### Audit steps

1. Save the input to a temp file: `INPUT_PATH=$(mktemp /tmp/human-eyes-input-XXXXXX.md)`. Write the draft to it.
2. Run the grader: `python3 grade.py --format json "$INPUT_PATH"`.
3. Parse the grader output. For each detected pattern, extract these fields: the pattern name; the offending phrase or sentence quoted from the input; the severity; and the relevant explanation from `references/patterns.md`.
4. Render the audit using the output template below.
5. End with the next-step question and stop without proceeding to a rewrite.

### Audit output

```
**Audit, N AI tells found**

<pattern-name-1> (M instances)
  - "<quoted phrase from input>"
  - "<another quoted phrase>"
  Why: <brief explanation, ~1-2 sentences>

<pattern-name-2> (M instances)
  - "<quoted phrase>"
  Why: <explanation>

<structural-pattern-name>
  Where: <description of where the pattern appears, e.g., "every paragraph in section 2 is 4-5 sentences and ends with a summary line">
  Why: <explanation>

...

**Confidence**

Render four fields from the grader: the level (low / medium / high / very high), the meaning string explaining what the level implies, the basis list summarising why, and a note that this assessment describes AI-writing signs rather than offering a verdict about who wrote the text.

**Next step**

Want Suggestions for per-flag replacements, a Rewrite at a chosen depth, or to save this audit as a Markdown file?
```

### Rendering rules

- Lexical patterns (specific words or phrases) get the `- "<quoted>"` shape.
- Structural patterns (paragraph-length uniformity, anaphoric scaffolding, section scaffolding, sentence-length variance) get a `Where:` line that describes where the pattern shows up and what it looks like in the draft. They have no single quotable instance to point at.
- Keep explanations concrete and avoid jargon. The point is to teach the writer how to recognise the pattern rather than display the catalogue.

If no patterns are detected, say so plainly:

> Audit clean: no AI tells detected. Want me to look at a specific aspect of the draft more closely?

If the grader is unavailable (no Python, restricted environment), fall back to a manual scan reading `references/patterns.md`. Disclose the limitation: a manual scan covers surface patterns and cannot replicate the script's structural and density checks.

---

## Action 2: Suggestions

### Suggestion steps

1. If an audit hasn't been run on this draft in this turn, run one first.
2. For each flagged pattern from the audit, produce one suggestion:
   - Lexical patterns (`delve`, hedging phrases, formulaic depth phrases, em dashes, AI vocabulary words): pull from `references/alternatives.md`. Pick the alternative that fits the surrounding sentence.
   - Structural patterns (paragraph-length uniformity, anaphoric scaffolding, sentence-length variance, tonal uniformity): compose a contextual suggestion. Substitution does not work for these patterns; rewriting does. Say what to vary and how.
3. Render the audit findings followed by the suggestions list.
4. Stop without producing a full rewrite.

### Suggestion output

```
[Audit output as in Action 1, but without the trailing question]

**Suggestions, one per flag**

<pattern-name-1>
  - "<quoted phrase from the audit>"
  Try: "<replacement>"

<pattern-name-2>
  - "<another quoted phrase>"
  Try: "<replacement>"

<structural-pattern>
  Try: <contextual rewriting instruction, e.g., "Vary paragraph 3 to be one short sentence, then expand paragraph 4 with two new clauses to break the rhythm.">

...

**Next step**

Want to apply these yourself, ask for a Rewrite at a chosen depth, or save this list of suggestions as a Markdown file?
```

The number of suggestions equals the number of flagged tells, with no filtering applied. The writer chooses what to apply.

---

## The depth dial (Rewrite and Write only)

Two settings:

- **Balanced** *(default)*: address surface-level and strong AI tells — lexical tells (such as `delve` and em dashes), formulaic openers, manufactured-insight framing, signposted conclusions, AI vocabulary clustering, contrived contrast, and soft scaffold phrasing. Most context warnings can be preserved if they are doing real work. Structural patterns like paragraph-length uniformity may remain.
- **All**: address every flagged pattern, even the implicit and structural ones a reader wouldn't consciously notice. Voice can take a hit at this depth. The goal is unambiguous human-shape; preserving every flourish comes second.

When the user has not chosen a depth, ask which depth they want. Balanced is the lighter pass; All is the deeper one. Default to Balanced if the writer declines to choose.

The dial only governs Rewrite and Write. It does not apply to Audit or Suggestions. Both of those return the full list of flagged tells regardless.

---

## Action 3: Rewrite

### Rewrite steps

1. Run the audit (as in Action 1) regardless of whether the writer asked directly. The audit findings are part of the Rewrite output, and the writer benefits from seeing what was flagged before reading the rewrite.
2. Pick the depth: ask if you can; default to Balanced. See the depth dial section above.
3. Read `references/voice.md` for voice-craft guidance. Recent research (Abdulhai et al., 2026) showed that LLM rewrites flatten stance and deplete pronouns; neutrality creeps in even on grammar-only passes. Voice-preservation is required during the rewrite.
4. Read `references/process.md`; it is required for Rewrite and Write. Address structural patterns first (Step A), then surface patterns (Step B).
5. Run the structural self-check (Step C from `process.md`). Show the answers in the output.
6. Run the semantic preservation check (Step D from `process.md`).
7. Re-grade the rewrite at the chosen depth. If required failures remain for that depth, revise before returning unless removing the issue would materially change meaning or voice. Iterate up to two re-runs and then report unresolved issues. Do not loop indefinitely.
8. Render the audit findings, the rewritten draft, and the post-check.

### Rewrite output

```
[Audit output as in Action 1, but without the trailing question]

**Depth: <Balanced / All>**
Why: <one sentence on why this depth fits the genre or the writer's stated preference>

**Rewrite**

<rewritten draft>

**Structural self-check**
1. Section arcs: <count of similar arcs>/<total>. <What you changed.>
2. Resolution density: <count of summary endings>/<total>. <What you changed.>
3. Register breaks: <where you added one, or "already present at...">
4. Triads: <count> found, reduced to <count> by <method>.
5. Reframe laundering: <none / fixed at...>
6. Purposeful devices preserved: <device: reason, or "none">
7. Stance: <preserved / shifted, restored by...>
8. Remaining tells: <list, or "none identified">

**Post-check at <depth>**
- Score: how many of the grader's checks failed at the chosen depth, plus whether AI-pressure triggered or stayed clear.
- Confidence: the level and meaning string from the grader, with the caveat that this describes AI-writing signs rather than offering an authorship verdict.
- Remaining issues: list the failed checks that remain unaddressed, or state "none" if the rewrite cleared everything.
```

Show only the final rewrite. Intermediate drafts stay hidden unless the writer asks. Do not modify the writer's source file.

---

## Action 4: Write

### Drafting steps

1. Pick the depth: ask if you can; default to Balanced.
2. Read `references/voice.md`. Write produces new prose, so voice-craft is load-bearing. There is no input voice to preserve; the writer fills an absence.
3. Read `references/process.md`; it is required for Rewrite and Write. Apply the structural patterns (Step A) prospectively while drafting. Vary paragraph structures and take a stance. Avoid the default section arc.
4. Draft from the brief at the chosen depth.
5. Run the structural self-check (Step C from `process.md`) on the draft.
6. Re-grade the draft at the chosen depth. If required failures remain for that depth, revise before returning unless fixing them would materially change the brief or voice. Iterate up to two re-runs, then report unresolved issues.
7. Render the draft and the post-check.

### Drafting output

```
**Brief: <one-sentence restatement of what the writer asked for>**

**Depth: <Balanced / All>**
Why: <one sentence on why this depth fits the brief>

**Draft**

<drafted text>

**Structural self-check**
[Same eight items as Rewrite]

**Post-check at <depth>**

/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:412:def split_sentences(text):
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:413:    """Split text into sentences (rough but usable)."""
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:414:    text = re.sub(r'\n+', ' ', text)
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:415:    sentences = re.split(r'(?<=[.!?])\s+', text)
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:416:    return [s.strip() for s in sentences if s.strip()]
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:417:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:418:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:419:def count_pattern_matches(text, patterns):
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:420:    """Count total matches of regex patterns in text (case-insensitive)."""
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:421:    text_lower = text.lower()
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:422:    total = 0
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:423:    matches = []
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:424:    for pat in patterns:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:425:        found = re.findall(pat, text_lower)
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:426:        if found:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:427:            total += len(found)
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:428:            matches.extend(found)
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:429:    return total, matches
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:430:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:431:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:432:def normalize_for_regex(text):
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:433:    """Normalize punctuation variants that agents use to dodge phrase checks."""
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:434:    return (
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:435:        text.lower()
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:436:        .replace("\u2019", "'")
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:437:        .replace("\u2018", "'")
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:438:        .replace("\u201c", '"')
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:439:        .replace("\u201d", '"')
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:440:        .replace("\u2014", " - ")
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:441:        .replace("\u2013", " - ")
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:442:    )
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:443:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:444:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:445:def word_counts(text):
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:446:    """Return lowercase word counts for exact vocabulary-set matching."""
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:447:    counts = {}
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:448:    for token in re.findall(r"\b[a-z][a-z0-9-]*\b", normalize_for_regex(text)):
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:449:        counts[token] = counts.get(token, 0) + 1
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:450:    return counts
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:451:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:452:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:453:def strip_front_matter(text):
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:454:    """Remove YAML front matter from markdown fixtures before prose checks."""
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:455:    if text.startswith("---\n"):
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:456:        parts = text.split("\n---\n", 1)
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:457:        if len(parts) == 2:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:458:            return parts[1]
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:459:    return text
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:460:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:461:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:462:def prose_paragraphs(text):
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:463:    """Return prose-like paragraphs, excluding fixture metadata and headings."""
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:464:    text = strip_front_matter(text)
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:465:    paragraphs = []
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:466:    for para in re.split(r"\n\s*\n", text):
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:467:        stripped = para.strip()
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:468:        if not stripped:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:469:            continue
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:470:        lines = [
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:471:            line.strip()
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:472:            for line in stripped.splitlines()
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:473:            if not re.match(r"^#{1,6}\s+", line.strip())
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:474:        ]
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:475:        joined = " ".join(line for line in lines if line)
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:476:        if joined:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:477:            paragraphs.append(joined)
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:478:    return paragraphs
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:479:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:480:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:481:# --- Checks ---
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:482:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:483:def check_em_dashes(text):
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:484:    count = text.count('\u2014')
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:485:    return {
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:486:        "text": "no-em-dashes",
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:487:        "passed": count == 0,
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:488:        "evidence": f"Found {count} em dash(es)" if count > 0 else "No em dashes found",

/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1151:    patterns = [

/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1213:def check_markdown_headings(text):
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1214:    """Detect markdown heading structure in prose (AI essays use ## sections)."""
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1215:    headings = []
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1216:    for match in re.finditer(r'^#{1,3}\s+.+', strip_front_matter(text), re.MULTILINE):
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1217:        heading = match.group()
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1218:        # Extracted source metadata often arrives as linked publication labels.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1219:        # Keep detecting article/essay section scaffolding, but do not punish
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1220:        # archive chrome such as "### [Issue 194, Fall 2010](...)".
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1221:        if re.match(r'^#{1,3}\s+\[[^\]]+\]\([^)]+\)\s*$', heading):
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1222:            continue
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1223:        headings.append(heading)
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1224:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1225:    lines = strip_front_matter(text).splitlines()
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1226:    first_nonblank = next((idx for idx, line in enumerate(lines) if line.strip()), None)
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1227:    if first_nonblank is not None and first_nonblank + 1 < len(lines):
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1228:        title = lines[first_nonblank].strip()
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1229:        followed_by_blank = not lines[first_nonblank + 1].strip()
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1230:        words = re.findall(r"[A-Za-z][A-Za-z'-]*", title)
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1231:        significant = [w for w in words if w.lower() not in {"a", "an", "and", "as", "at", "by", "for", "in", "of", "on", "or", "the", "to", "with"}]
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1232:        title_case_words = sum(1 for w in significant if w[0].isupper())
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1233:        looks_like_plain_title = (
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1234:            followed_by_blank
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1235:            and 3 <= len(words) <= 12
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1236:            and len(title) <= 90
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1237:            and not re.search(r"[.!?]$", title)
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1238:            and not title.startswith(("[", "{", "("))
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1239:            and title_case_words >= max(2, len(significant) - 1)
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1240:        )
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1241:        if looks_like_plain_title:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1242:            headings.append(title)
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1243:    return {
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1244:        "text": "no-markdown-headings",
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1245:        "passed": len(headings) == 0,
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1246:        "evidence": (
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1247:            f"Found {len(headings)} heading(s): {[h[:50] for h in headings[:5]]}"
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1248:            if headings
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1249:            else "No headings"
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1250:        ),

/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1282:def check_this_chains(text):
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1283:    """Detect 3+ consecutive sentences starting with 'This [verb]'."""
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1284:    paragraphs = [p.strip() for p in text.split('\n\n') if p.strip()]
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1285:    worst_run = 0
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1286:    for para in paragraphs:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1287:        sentences = split_sentences(para)
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1288:        current_run = 0
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1289:        for s in sentences:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1290:            if re.match(r'^this\s+(?!is\b)\w+', s.strip().lower()):
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1291:                current_run += 1
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1292:                worst_run = max(worst_run, current_run)
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1293:            else:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1294:                current_run = 0
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1295:    return {
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1296:        "text": "no-this-chains",
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1297:        "passed": worst_run < 3,
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1298:        "evidence": (
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1299:            f"Found {worst_run} consecutive 'This [verb]' sentences"
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1300:            if worst_run >= 3
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1301:            else f"Max 'This [verb]' run: {worst_run}"
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1302:        ),
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1303:    }
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1304:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1305:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1306:def check_countdown_negation(text):
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1307:    """Detect dramatic countdown negation.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1308:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1309:    Branch 1: 'It wasn't X. It wasn't Y. It was Z.' - 2+ negation sentences
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1310:    with it/this/that followed by an affirmative reveal.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1311:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1312:    Branch 2: 'You can't X. You can't Y. You can't Z.' - 3+ consecutive
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1313:    sentences where the same subject pronoun is followed by a negated verb.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1314:    No affirmative reveal required. Threshold is 3 because 2 consecutive
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1315:    same-subject negations are common in opinion writing.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1316:    """
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1317:    # Branch 1: existing countdown-then-reveal pattern (do not change)
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1318:    pattern = r'(?:(?:it|this|that) (?:wasn\'t|isn\'t|was not|is not) [^.?!]+[.]\s*){2,}(?:it|this|that) (?:was|is) [^.?!]+[.]'
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1319:    matches = re.findall(pattern, text.lower())
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1320:    if matches:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1321:        return {
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1322:            "text": "no-countdown-negation",
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1323:            "passed": False,
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1324:            "evidence": f"Found {len(matches)} countdown negation sequence(s)",
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1325:        }
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1326:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1327:    # Branch 2: 3+ consecutive same-subject pronoun-negation sentences
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1328:    negated_verbs = r"(?:can't|won't|don't|shouldn't|couldn't|cannot|will not|do not|should not|could not)"
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1329:    subjects = ("you", "we", "they", "people")
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1330:    sentences = split_sentences(text)
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1331:    max_run = 0
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1332:    current_run = 0
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1333:    current_subject = None
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1334:    for s in sentences:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1335:        s_lower = s.strip().lower()
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1336:        matched_subject = None
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1337:        for subj in subjects:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1338:            if re.match(rf'^{subj}\s+{negated_verbs}\b', s_lower):
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1339:                matched_subject = subj
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1340:                break
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1341:        if matched_subject and matched_subject == current_subject:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1342:            current_run += 1

/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1351:    if max_run >= 3:

/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1536:def check_section_scaffolding(text):
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1537:    """Detect repeated identical subheadings across sections (pattern 38)."""
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1538:    lines = text.split('\n')
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1539:    counts = {}
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1540:    for line in lines:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1541:        stripped = line.strip()
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1542:        # Strip leading markdown heading markers
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1543:        stripped = re.sub(r'^#+\s*', '', stripped)
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1544:        normalised = stripped.lower().strip()
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1545:        # Skip empty lines and lines that are only punctuation/markdown markers
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1546:        if not normalised or re.match(r'^[#*_\-=~`>|]+$', normalised):
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1547:            continue
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1548:        # Only count short lines (under 60 chars): these are labels, not prose
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1549:        if len(normalised) < 60:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1550:            counts[normalised] = counts.get(normalised, 0) + 1
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1551:    # Flag if any normalised line appears 3+ times
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1552:    repeated = {label: n for label, n in counts.items() if n >= 3}
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1553:    if repeated:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1554:        worst = max(repeated, key=repeated.get)
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1555:        return {
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1556:            "text": "no-section-scaffolding",
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1557:            "passed": False,

/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1567:def check_hedging_density(text):
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1568:    """Detect excessive impersonal passive hedging density."""
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1569:    paragraphs = [p.strip() for p in text.split('\n\n') if p.strip()]
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1570:    total_matches = 0
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1571:    all_found = []
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1572:    for para in paragraphs:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1573:        para_lower = para.lower()
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1574:        for pat in HEDGING_PATTERNS:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1575:            found = re.findall(pat, para_lower)
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1576:            if found:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1577:                total_matches += len(found)
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1578:                all_found.extend(found)
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1579:    # Flag at 4+ hedging constructions across the whole text
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1580:    return {
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1581:        "text": "no-excessive-hedging",
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1582:        "passed": total_matches < 4,
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1583:        "evidence": (
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1584:            f"Found {total_matches} hedging constructions: {all_found[:5]}"
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1585:            if total_matches >= 4
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1586:            else f"Hedging constructions: {total_matches}"
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1587:        ),
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1588:    }
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:1589:

/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2130:def confidence_assessment(results):
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2131:    """Assess confidence that the text shows AI-writing patterns, not authorship."""
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2132:    summary = score_summary(results)
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2133:    failures = summary["failed_checks"]
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2134:    severities = summary["failures_by_severity"]
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2135:    ai_pressure = summary.get("ai_signal_pressure") or {}
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2136:    hard = severities.get("hard_fail", 0)
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2137:    strong = severities.get("strong_warning", 0)
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2138:    context = severities.get("context_warning", 0)
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2139:    pressure_triggered = ai_pressure.get("triggered", False)
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2140:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2141:    if failures == 0:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2142:        level = "Low"
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2143:        explanation = "No programmatic checks showed AI-writing patterns."
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2144:    elif hard or strong >= 3 or (pressure_triggered and failures >= 6) or failures >= 8:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2145:        level = "High"
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2146:        explanation = (
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2147:            "Multiple strong or structural signals fired. Treat this as a high-confidence "
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2148:            "style diagnosis, not proof of authorship."
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2149:        )

/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2361:def score_summary(results):
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2362:    """Return structured totals for human-facing reports."""
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2363:    passed = sum(1 for result in results if result["passed"])
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2364:    total = len(results)
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2365:    failures = [result for result in results if not result["passed"]]
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2366:    failures_by_severity = {}
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2367:    for result in failures:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2368:        failures_by_severity[result["severity"]] = failures_by_severity.get(result["severity"], 0) + 1
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2369:

/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2515:def _section_text(output_text, header_re):
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2516:    """Return the text of a section starting at header_re, ending at the next bold header."""
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2517:    match = header_re.search(output_text)
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2518:    if not match:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2519:        return None
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2520:    start = match.end()
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2521:    next_match = SECTION_HEADER_RE.search(output_text, start)
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2522:    end = next_match.start() if next_match else len(output_text)
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2523:    return output_text[start:end]

/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2550:def check_audit_shape_block_precedes_rewrite_block(output_text, input_text=None):
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2551:    """Verify the audit appears before any rewrite or suggestions block."""
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2552:    name = "audit-shape-block-precedes-rewrite-block"
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2553:    audit = AUDIT_HEADER_RE.search(output_text)
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2554:    rewrite = REWRITE_HEADER_RE.search(output_text) or DRAFT_HEADER_RE.search(output_text)
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2555:    suggestions = SUGGESTIONS_HEADER_RE.search(output_text)
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2556:    if rewrite and suggestions:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2557:        follow = rewrite if rewrite.start() < suggestions.start() else suggestions
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2558:    else:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2559:        follow = rewrite or suggestions
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2560:    if not audit:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2561:        return {"text": name, "passed": False, "evidence": "No audit header found in output"}
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2562:    if not follow:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2563:        return {"text": name, "passed": False, "evidence": "No rewrite, draft, or suggestions header found in output"}
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2564:    if audit.start() < follow.start():
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2565:        return {"text": name, "passed": True, "evidence": f"audit at offset {audit.start()}; follow-up at offset {follow.start()}"}
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2566:    return {"text": name, "passed": False, "evidence": f"follow-up appears before audit (audit={audit.start()}, follow={follow.start()})"}

/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2569:def check_every_flag_block_contains_input_substring(output_text, input_text=None):
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2570:    """Each flag block must quote a phrase that appears in the input."""
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2571:    name = "every-flag-block-contains-input-substring"
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2572:    if input_text is None:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2573:        return {"text": name, "passed": False, "evidence": "input_text required for this check"}
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2574:    blocks = _flag_blocks(_audit_section(output_text))
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2575:    if not blocks:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2576:        return {"text": name, "passed": True, "evidence": "no flag blocks (vacuously true)"}
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2577:    misses = []
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2578:    for block in blocks:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2579:        if re.search(r"^\s*Where\s*:", block, re.MULTILINE):
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2580:            continue  # structural patterns describe location, not quotable phrase
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2581:        phrases = QUOTED_PHRASE_RE.findall(block)
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2582:        if not phrases or not any(phrase in input_text for phrase in phrases):

/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2680:    """Re-grade prose at a chosen depth.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2681:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2682:    Returns a dict with depth-aware fail counts so an eval grader can verify

/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/grade.py:2684:

/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:1:1. Servant Hedonism: My Life Philosophy
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:2:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:3:One Sunday afternoon, stretched out on the couch with a book, it struck me that I had been living by a philosophy without ever naming it.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:4:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:5:It isn’t elaborate. It doesn’t rest on an all-encompassing theory of history. It offers no checklist of rules or algorithm for decision-making.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:6:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:7:Yet it has proven reliable. In periods when everything felt unstable or overwhelming, it gave me orientation.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:8:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:9:I call it Servant Hedonism — serving yourself by serving others.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:10:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:11:It brings together two concepts that are usually framed as opposites: service and pleasure.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:12:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:13:Begin with “servant.”
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:14:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:15:By this I mean something close to Servant Leadership: the idea that life is best approached through steady, unglamorous generosity — a commitment to be useful where possible, as often as possible.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:16:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:17:When I’ve faced difficult choices, this orientation has repeatedly pointed toward the most meaningful outcomes. Its strength lies in its simplicity. Most of the time, it is obvious where help is needed. And opportunities to contribute are never scarce.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:18:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:19:To serve is to adopt a humble stance toward existence. You arise from a web of relationships and conditions, and you will return to it. You are responsible for sustaining yourself. But that sustenance can come through contribution rather than extraction.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:20:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:21:Put differently, you are not a self-contained unit. You are part of a lineage and a community that precedes you and will outlast you. During your brief interval here, you can add something of value.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:22:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:23:There are pragmatic advantages as well. Service generates positive-sum exchanges. It builds trust. It aligns people around shared efforts.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:24:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:25:Even so, the most compelling reason to serve is simpler: it feels good.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:26:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:27:This brings us to “hedonism,” which matters even more.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:28:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:29:Being of service is the most deeply satisfying state I know. Few experiences compare to offering your attention or skill to someone and witnessing the moment something shifts for them. In a culture saturated with self-absorption, it feels like a rare privilege.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:30:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:31:But service alone can harden into self-denial. The insistence on being useful can slip into martyrdom or quiet burnout. A rigid focus on contribution can obscure one’s own worth.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:32:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:33:As adrienne maree brown observes, “You cannot create freedom for others through your own bondage. You cannot empower others through your own demoralization. You cannot create a fulfilling life for others by draining your own of its color. You are a seed, and that is not how seeds work.”
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:34:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:35:Without pleasure, service becomes another discipline of control.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:36:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:37:Service and pleasure are frequently treated as competing commitments, as though attention to one diminishes the other. As though enjoyment necessarily undermines generosity.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:38:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:39:I reject that framing.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:40:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:41:The two can reinforce each other. Asking how to contribute shapes you into someone you respect. Allowing yourself enjoyment — including your imperfections — increases your capacity to give.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:42:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:43:At its core, Servant Hedonism is an ethic of self-regard.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:44:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:45:It means counting yourself among those who deserve care. It means refusing to accept chronic depletion as a moral badge. It means asking, as brown does, “What is happening and why did I decide to endure it?”
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:46:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:47:Charles Eisenstein writes, “Pleasure is the feeling we get from satisfying a need. The more powerful the need, the greater the pleasure. To follow this principle requires, first, accepting that our needs are valid and even beautiful. And not just our needs, but our desires as well, coming as they do from unmet needs.”
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:48:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:49:Once you collapse the boundary between serving and enjoying, it becomes difficult to distinguish whose needs are being met. The binary between giver and receiver softens.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:50:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:51:There is a contagious quality to this approach. When people experience safety, belonging, intimacy and connection, they tend to extend the same outward. The impulse to serve emerges naturally.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:52:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:53:The word “hedonism” unsettles some. It suggests indulgence without restraint, excess at others’ expense. I retain it deliberately.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:54:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:55:Your own well-being must remain central. If your life is treated purely as an instrument for some external objective, then human life itself ceases to be the highest value. And when that hierarchy shifts, it becomes easy to justify using others as means.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:56:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:57:Servant Hedonism draws people in not through guilt or obligation but through visible vitality. Joy is persuasive. People gravitate toward what appears alive.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:58:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:59:There is no need for coercion. Pleasure exerts its own pull. It sustains motion without constant force.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:60:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:61:Pleasure can function as an organising principle for a life that generates more than it consumes. It can shape activism, institutions and economies. It may be among the few principles that does not exhaust itself over time.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:62:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:63:Adopting this stance is not effortless. At first it can be uncomfortable.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:64:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:65:There is a fear of losing discipline, of sliding into unrestrained self-indulgence. Many of us have internalised the belief that suffering carries moral weight, while enjoyment is suspect. We imagine that we must first eliminate all personal lack before we can justify generosity. But needs proliferate.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:66:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:67:What steadies me is a simple reminder: you are allowed to inhabit the future you are trying to build. You do not need to sacrifice yourself to make it real. Your freedom and satisfaction are not obstacles to collective flourishing; they are part of it.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:68:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:69:Thank you to Ben Ford, Javier Rodriguez, and Michael Ashcroft for their feedback and suggestions.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:70:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:71:2
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:72:Workshops as Portals
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:73:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:74:And how to create clarity in consulting work
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:75:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:76:Selling consulting work is structurally awkward. There’s an inherent inside–outside tension: how much can be known about a client and their problem before a proposal is written?
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:77:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:78:The task, in practice, is to find a way to move from the outside in. To get close enough to the work to understand it properly—without already being embedded.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:79:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:80:A workshop does that.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:81:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:82:Over the past few years, the default response to new enquiries has been to propose a workshop first. It has consistently led to stronger engagements and longer retention.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:83:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:84:“Workshop” is a broad term. Here, it refers specifically to sessions designed to produce clarity—a way to step inside the client’s context and establish a basis for ongoing work.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:85:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:86:The key shift is to stop treating the workshop as a vehicle for solving problems. Instead, treat it as a working session that sharpens understanding.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:87:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:88:Trying to resolve something like brand strategy in a single day is unrealistic. Establishing clarity around it is not.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:89:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:90:And clarity has economic value. Structured correctly, these sessions can be priced accordingly.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:91:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:92:This piece covers:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:93:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:94:* Why clarity matters to both sides
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:95:* How to structure workshops for outcomes rather than experience
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:96:* Conversation prompts that support productive sessions
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:97:* Why pricing should not be discounted (e.g. $4,000/day)
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:98:* Examples drawn from practice
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:99:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:100:First, a framing device.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:101:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:102:In media res
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:103:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:104:“In media res” means starting in the middle of the action. It’s a common narrative technique—begin with movement, then fill in context.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:105:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:106:It works because people tolerate exposition once they have a sense of momentum.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:107:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:108:Consulting follows a similar pattern. Both parties want early evidence of what working together actually feels like.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:109:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:110:From the client’s perspective:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:111:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:112:* Can this person address our specific issues?
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:113:* Do they demonstrate competence?
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:114:* Will our ways of working align?
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:115:* Are they reliable?
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:116:* How do I justify this internally?
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:117:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:118:From the consultant’s perspective:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:119:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:120:* Is there sufficient scope here?
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:121:* What are the underlying problems?
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:122:* What are the team dynamics?
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:123:* Will this relationship work?
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:124:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:125:This is the core tension. Proposals are expected before sufficient context exists, and client problem statements are often incomplete or inaccurate.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:126:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:127:At the same time, the process typically begins with pricing and contractual discussion—before either party has enough signal.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:128:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:129:The question becomes: how to approximate in media res early, without committing to a full engagement?
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:130:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:131:Selling portal workshops
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:132:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:133:One practical approach is to respond to initial conversations with a defined workshop—a “portal” into the organisation.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:134:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:135:A limited, contained piece of work is usually easier to approve than a retainer or large programme. It provides access to real context without requiring full commitment.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:136:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:137:Notably, the scope is deliberately loose. These sessions are not heavily programmed. They function better as exploratory working sessions than tightly scripted workshops.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:138:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:139:Jam sessions
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:140:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:141:“Workshop” carries assumptions—facilitation plans, structured activities, predefined outputs.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:142:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:143:In this model, the format is closer to a collaborative session than a facilitated sequence. Preparation might range from a minimal agenda to a short framing deck, but excessive structure tends to reduce effectiveness.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:144:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:145:In practice, the session behaves more like a jam session: adaptive, conversational, and responsive.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:146:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:147:The objective is to surface enough context to answer key unknowns:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:148:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:149:* Establish working rapport
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:150:* Signal attentiveness and listening
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:151:* Identify the real problems—and whether budget exists to address them
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:152:* Demonstrate capability
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:153:* Observe team dynamics
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:154:* Understand context (what, who, why now)
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:155:* Surface blind spots
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:156:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:157:These are not trivial. They require space for divergence and follow-up questioning.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:158:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:159:Group size matters. More than five or six participants reduces signal quality. Larger groups introduce performance behaviour and suppress dissent. Smaller groups tend to produce more accurate information.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:160:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:161:Conversation prompts for running an effective jam session
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:162:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:163:Unstructured does not mean unprepared. The session still requires deliberate framing.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:164:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:165:Useful prompts and techniques include:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:166:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:167:* Start by restating the situation and objectives. Test for inconsistencies and push for specificity.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:168:* Operate iteratively rather than linearly. Follow threads rather than progressing through a fixed sequence.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:169:* Prioritise simple questions over clever ones. For example: “Why wouldn’t we approach this the same way as the previous project?” or “If the problem and solution are clear, what role is needed here?”
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:170:* Use hypotheticals to expose assumptions. “If a senior hire were already in place, what would they prioritise?”
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:171:* Be direct. “Do you have the capability to execute this?” or “What is preventing approval?”
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:172:* Resolve small, contained issues where appropriate to build credibility.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:173:* Avoid resolving large issues prematurely. Early “solutions” are often contextually incomplete or already rejected internally.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:174:* Reframe problems explicitly to test understanding.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:175:* Maintain a visible list of discrete issues—this becomes input for subsequent scoping.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:176:* Allow silence. Delays in response often indicate real complexity rather than disengagement.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:177:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:178:Engineer for outcomes not optimism
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:179:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:180:The aim is not a positive experience. It is a productive one.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:181:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:182:This distinction matters. Effective sessions often feel demanding. They challenge assumptions and expose gaps.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:183:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:184:Clients tend to value sessions that produce movement—even if uncomfortable—over sessions that are smooth but inconsequential.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:185:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:186:Define the outcomes required in advance and drive towards them.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:187:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:188:Workshops for selling open-ended retainers
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:189:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:190:Post-workshop, several outcomes typically emerge:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:191:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:192:* Established working relationship
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:193:* Shared experience of collaboration
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:194:* Improved understanding of context and constraints
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:195:* Initial framing of the problem space
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:196:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:197:One additional effect is less obvious:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:198:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:199:Detailed statements of work become easier to agree.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:200:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:201:Without context, both parties attempt to specify future work precisely, which leads to friction. After a workshop, shared understanding reduces this need.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:202:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:203:Scopes can remain lightweight. For example:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:204:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:205:* On-site support for audience development, two days per week
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:206:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:207:The reduction in specification overhead is significant.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:208:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:209:$4,000 days — a note on pricing
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:210:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:211:There is a temptation to discount these sessions, given their role in securing longer-term work.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:212:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:213:This is counterproductive.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:214:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:215:The workshop sets the pricing anchor. Lowering the rate here makes it difficult to increase it later.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:216:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:217:A common approach is to price at approximately double the standard day rate. For example, a $2,000 day rate becomes a $4,000 workshop.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:218:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:219:This remains a relatively small commitment for the client while signalling value. It can be justified through preparation, synthesis, and follow-up.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:220:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:221:A common failure mode is overcompensating for price by over-structuring the session. This reduces effectiveness.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:222:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:223:The key point: the session is not solving the problem.

/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:231:A two-day workshop was agreed following a single call and a one-page outline. The agenda was minimal.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:232:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/rewritten ai essays.txt:233:This led directly to a multi-year retainer.
