/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:462:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:463:▎ My Life Philosophy
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:464:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:465:It was an unremarkable Sunday afternoon when it landed. The laundry was half-done. The week’s calendar was open. I was doing that familiar thing where “rest” quietly turns into “planning” and the mind starts tallying what it owes.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:466:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:467:I began a list—who to check in on, what to finish, what to organise—and noticed the list wasn’t really about tasks. It was about what I keep trying to protect: people, promises, momentum, and whatever version of myself can meet the world without bracing.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:468:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:469:That’s when it clicked: there is a life philosophy running under my habits. It’s been there for years, shaping my choices even when I haven’t named it.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:470:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:471:If it needs a name, it’s Servant Hedonism.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:472:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:473:Here’s the thesis. Service to others and personal pleasure are usually treated as opposites. The cultural script is neat: sacrifice equals goodness; enjoyment equals selfishness. Pick a side.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:474:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:475:But in lived reality they’re not enemies. They reinforce each other. Service without pleasure becomes martyrdom. Pleasure without service becomes self-centredness. Together, deliberately, they form something stable: a way of living that is both useful and vivid.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:476:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:477:This isn’t a pitch for hustle in disguise, and it isn’t a defence of constant indulgence. It’s a practical philosophy for building a life where contribution and enjoyment support the same system: your energy, attention, and capacity to show up.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:478:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:479:The servant half
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:480:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:481:Service is the most reliable compass I have. When I feel scattered, the fastest way back is to ask: Who needs something real from me, and what does “help” actually mean right now?
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:482:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:483:There’s a steadiness that comes from being useful. It shifts me out of my own internal commentary and into concrete action: cook a meal, make the call, review the document carefully, show up, follow through. It also forces contact with reality. Reality has constraints—time, money, bodies, consent. If I’m paying attention, service trains humility: not self-neglect, but accurate self-assessment.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:484:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:485:Service is also a powerful productivity anchor. It turns effort into meaning. It makes planning feel less like personal optimisation and more like stewardship: of relationships, teams, and work that matters beyond my ego.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:486:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:487:But service has a shadow, and it’s not subtle once you know the signs.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:488:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:489:For me, the shadow is the moment service stops being chosen and becomes performed. Saying yes because I’m afraid of disappointing people. Confusing being needed with being valued. Treating my capacity as infinite and my needs as negotiable.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:490:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:491:Martyrdom doesn’t always look dramatic. Often it looks competent. Reliable. “Fine.” It’s the slow leak of always being the one who absorbs the mess, stays late, answers quickly, smooths things over, and then wonders why life feels tight and grey.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:492:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:493:The clearest early warning is resentment. Resentment is data. It says: Something here is out of balance. You’re giving beyond what you can sustainably offer.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:494:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:495:Another trap is control. Constant helping can be a way to avoid being helped. If I’m always the capable one, I don’t have to risk receiving, admitting limits, or letting someone else do it differently.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:496:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:497:This is where adrienne maree brown’s work has been a useful corrective. She writes about liberation and care in a way that refuses the idea that other people’s freedom should be built on someone else’s ongoing bondage. I treat that as an audit question: Am I trying to do good in a way that makes me smaller? If yes, it’s not noble. It’s a design flaw.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:498:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:499:Service is essential. But service alone becomes a moral treadmill: always more to do, always someone else first, always a lingering sense that rest must be earned.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:500:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:501:The hedonist half
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:502:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:503:If service is my compass, pleasure is my fuel. And for a long time, I distrusted fuel. Pleasure felt like something to permit only if it didn’t interfere with output.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:504:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:505:That approach is efficient in the short term. It’s also brittle. A life that treats enjoyment as optional eventually pays for it in the currency of low motivation, thin creativity, and a constant sense of “getting through” rather than living.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:506:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:507:Pleasure, taken seriously, is not laziness. It’s information. It tells me what makes me feel awake. It points to what I care about when I’m not performing usefulness. It reminds me I’m a person in a body, not a productivity unit.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:508:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:509:It’s also something I can practise. In my best weeks, pleasure isn’t a reward at the end of deprivation; it’s built into the design. Coffee outside. Music while cleaning. A walk that isn’t for steps. Cooking slowly because it tastes better. A book that isn’t “relevant”. A proper laugh with someone who doesn’t require me to be impressive.

/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:511:In a productivity-adjacent sense, pleasure is maintenance. It replenishes attention. It reduces friction. /Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:512:

Other people become either an audience or an inconvenience. The world becomes a background for personal taste.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:516:

When pleasure is the primary organising principle, it can slide from aliveness into craving—more stimulation, more novelty, more consumption—while leaving the actual self untouched.

/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:521:Bringing them together: Servant Hedonism
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:522:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:523:Servant Hedonism is the decision to stop pretending these forces have to compete.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:524:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:525:Service keeps pleasure from becoming narcissism. Pleasure keeps service from becoming martyrdom.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:526:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:527:Together they form a feedback loop that works:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:528:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:529:	•	Service gives me a reason to care for myself, because my commitments require a functioning nervous system.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:530:	•	Pleasure gives me the capacity to keep serving, because it restores energy and goodwill.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:531:	•	Service expands meaning, which makes pleasure richer.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:532:	•	Pleasure softens my edges, which makes service kinder and less controlling.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:533:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:534:The day-to-day practice is small and repetitive. When I’m deciding how to spend an evening, or what to say yes to, I try to ask a paired question: What would be genuinely helpful here? And what would be genuinely nourishing? If I can’t answer both, I’m usually drifting towards one of the failure modes—self-sacrifice dressed up as virtue, or self-soothing dressed up as freedom.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:535:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:536:This philosophy also changes how I measure “goodness”.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:537:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:538:In the martyr model, goodness is self-erasure: the less I need, the better I am. In the indulgence model, goodness is self-gratification: the more I can secure, the better I am.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:539:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:540:In Servant Hedonism, goodness is sustainability with joy. Giving without disappearing. Receiving without taking. Contributing because I care, not because I’m buying worthiness with depletion.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:541:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:542:Practically, it shows up as a few rules I try to live by:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:543:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:544:	1	Resentment triggers renegotiation. Not because service is always easy, but because persistent resentment means I’m overdrawn.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:545:	2	Numbing isn’t pleasure. If a “treat” leaves me less present afterwards, it’s a coping strategy, not replenishment.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:546:	3	Helpful doesn’t mean indispensable. If my help makes me the only person who can hold something together, I’m probably propping up a broken system.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:547:	4	Joy is a line item. If my calendar has no joy, my service will eventually degrade into brittle efficiency.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:548:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:549:That Sunday afternoon wasn’t about discovering a new idea. It was about naming the pattern I keep returning to when life gets noisy.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:550:

The aim is to stop letting it split me into two incompatible selves: the “responsible” one who never rests, and the “free” one who doesn’t care.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:552:

/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:556:First men, original sins
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:557:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:558:Faith, frontier, and the moral imagination of American spaceflight
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:559:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:560:There is a familiar way to tell the story of the American space program: a sequence of technical milestones underwritten by Cold War urgency, culminating in the controlled triumph of Apollo 11 Moon Landing. The story moves from fear to mastery, from Sputnik to the Sea of Tranquillity, and it ends—implicitly—with a kind of secular apotheosis. Humanity leaves the earth, plants a flag, and returns with samples and images that seem to close the gap between myth and machine.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:561:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:562:Yet this account is incomplete. It omits the dense field of meaning that surrounded these events as they unfolded: the language of calling and destiny, the moral vocabulary of sacrifice and risk, the persistent intuition that to leave the earth was not merely to extend human capability but to trespass into a domain long reserved for the divine. Three works—To Touch the Face of God by Kendrick Oliver, Destined for the Stars by Catherine L. Newell, and First Man directed by Damien Chazelle—offer a more complex interpretive frame. Together, they show that American spaceflight was never merely technological. It was, from the outset, entangled with religious imagination, haunted by ideas of sin and transgression, and sustained by a frontier mythology that recast exploration as both redemption and repetition.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:563:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:564:What emerges is not a coherent theology of spaceflight but a set of overlapping, sometimes contradictory narratives: space as sacred frontier, as profane extension of earthly ambition, and as a site where the moral limits of human aspiration are tested.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:565:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:566:⸻
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:567:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:568:The sacred and the profane
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:569:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:570:Oliver’s central claim is deceptively simple: that religion was not peripheral to the American space program but constitutive of its meaning. His title, To Touch the Face of God, taken from a line often (mis)attributed to pilots and later associated with astronauts, captures the ambiguity at the heart of the enterprise. Is spaceflight an act of reverence or of hubris? Does it bring humanity closer to the divine, or does it risk collapsing the distinction altogether?
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:571:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:572:Oliver tracks how religious language permeated NASA’s culture and public representation. Astronauts were framed not only as test pilots but as exemplars of moral character—disciplined, self-sacrificing, and oriented towards a purpose that exceeded individual ambition. This framing mattered. In a period defined by existential threat, the figure of the astronaut provided a counter-image: not the anonymous bureaucrat of nuclear deterrence, but the visible, almost liturgical figure of the explorer who risked death in the pursuit of knowledge.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:573:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:574:Public rituals reinforced this framing. The reading from Genesis during Apollo 8 is a canonical example. As the spacecraft circled the Moon, the astronauts broadcast the opening lines of scripture to a global audience. Oliver notes the careful calibration of this moment: it was not a declaration of doctrine but an invocation of shared cultural language. The effect was nonetheless profound. The Moon—long a symbol in religious and poetic traditions—was recast as both object of scientific inquiry and stage for a quasi-liturgical performance.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:575:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:576:Yet Oliver resists any simple reading of this as evidence of a unified “space religion”. Instead, he emphasises tension. The same programme that staged moments of reverence also operated within a technocratic framework that prioritised control, calculation, and efficiency. The sacred and the profane were not reconciled; they coexisted uneasily.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:577:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:578:This tension becomes more acute when framed in terms of sin. If spaceflight is imagined as a reaching towards the divine, it risks repeating the archetypal narrative of overreach—the desire to transcend human limits. Oliver gestures towards this without fully resolving it. The language of transcendence, he suggests, always carries the shadow of transgression.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:579:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:580:⸻
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:581:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:582:Destiny and its discontents
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:583:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:584:If Oliver’s account is attentive to the ambiguity of religious language, Newell’s is more explicitly concerned with its forward projection. Destined for the Stars examines how religious imagination shaped not only the interpretation of early spaceflight but the expectations attached to its future.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:585:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:586:Newell argues that American space discourse drew heavily on eschatological motifs—ideas about ultimate destiny, fulfilment, and transformation. Space was not merely a new domain to explore; it was imagined as the next stage in a broader narrative of human development. This narrative was often framed in explicitly religious terms. Space colonisation became, in some accounts, a form of salvation: a way to transcend the limitations of earth, to escape ecological or geopolitical catastrophe, and to realise a latent potential embedded in human nature.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:587:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:588:This language intersects with frontier mythology. The American frontier has long been imagined as a space of renewal—a place where old constraints fall away and new identities can be forged. Newell shows how this mythology was transposed into a cosmic register. The “final frontier” is not simply the last in a series of geographic expansions; it is a reactivation of a familiar narrative in a new context.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:589:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:590:There is, however, a structural problem with this transposition. The frontier myth depends on a particular moral framing: expansion is justified because it is assumed to bring progress, order, and ultimately moral improvement. When applied to space, this assumption becomes harder to sustain. There are no indigenous populations on the Moon to displace, but the logic of expansion remains. The question is not who is harmed, but whether the underlying impulse—to extend control indefinitely—is itself subject to critique.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:591:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:592:Newell is attentive to this tension. She notes that alongside narratives of destiny, there were persistent counter-narratives that framed spaceflight as ethically ambiguous or even dangerous. Religious critiques often focused on the risk of idolatry: the elevation of technological achievement to a status that displaces or distorts traditional understandings of the divine.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:593:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:594:In this sense, spaceflight becomes a site where competing theologies of progress collide. One presents expansion as fulfilment; the other warns that the desire to transcend limits may itself be a form of moral failure.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:595:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:596:⸻
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:597:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:598:Silence, grief, and the limits of transcendence
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:599:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:600:Where Oliver and Newell work primarily through historical and textual analysis, First Man offers a different entry point. Chazelle’s film, centred on Neil Armstrong, is notable for what it withholds. It avoids overt theological framing and largely sidesteps the triumphant rhetoric associated with the space race. Instead, it presents spaceflight as an extension of a more intimate narrative: the attempt to live with loss.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:601:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:602:Armstrong’s journey in the film is structured around the death of his daughter. This loss is not resolved through the mission; it is carried into it. The Moon landing, in this context, is not a moment of transcendence in the usual sense. It is quieter, more ambiguous. The famous images are reframed through a lens of isolation. The lunar surface is not presented as a site of conquest but as an environment of radical distance—silent, indifferent, and resistant to projection.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:603:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:604:This reframing has implications for how the film engages with religious imagination. Rather than staging explicit moments of reverence, it allows the viewer to confront the limits of meaning. The question is not whether space brings humanity closer to the divine, but whether such a question can be meaningfully posed in a context defined by absence.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:605:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:606:There is a subtle but important shift here. In Oliver’s account, religious language provides a framework for interpreting spaceflight. In Newell’s, it shapes expectations about its future. In First Man, that framework begins to dissolve. The film suggests that the experience of space may resist the categories used to describe it. The sacred/profane distinction becomes less stable, not because it is resolved, but because it is rendered insufficient.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:607:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:608:The film’s final sequence, often read as symbolic, underscores this point. Armstrong leaves a personal token on the lunar surface—a gesture that has been variously interpreted as mourning, closure, or offering. The act is private, almost hidden within the broader narrative. It does not resolve the tension between transcendence and limitation; it simply marks it.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:609:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:610:⸻
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:611:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:612:Frontier myth and original sin
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:613:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:614:Across these works, the concept of the frontier operates as a unifying thread. It provides a narrative structure through which spaceflight is made intelligible: exploration leads to discovery, which leads to expansion, which leads to transformation. This structure is deeply embedded in American cultural history. It is also, as historians have long noted, morally ambivalent.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:615:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:616:The frontier myth carries within it a logic of repetition. Each new frontier is framed as an opportunity to correct the failures of the previous one—to build a more just, more equitable, more enlightened society. At the same time, the mechanisms of expansion often reproduce the very dynamics they are meant to overcome.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:617:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:618:When applied to space, this dynamic becomes abstract but no less significant. The absence of immediate ethical conflict does not eliminate the underlying question: what is the moral status of expansion itself? Is the drive to extend human presence indefinitely a form of fulfilment, or does it risk replicating a pattern of overreach that has historically been associated with harm?
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:619:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:620:This is where the language of sin becomes analytically useful. Not in a narrowly doctrinal sense, but as a way of naming the tension between aspiration and limitation. The desire to explore, to know, to extend—these are not inherently problematic. They are, in many accounts, constitutive of what it means to be human. The problem arises when these desires are framed as limitless, when the possibility of constraint is excluded from the narrative.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:621:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:622:Oliver’s attention to the coexistence of sacred and profane language highlights this tension. Newell’s analysis of destiny narratives shows how it is projected into the future. First Man renders it at the level of individual experience. Together, they suggest that the space program can be read as a site where the moral imagination of mid-century America is both expressed and tested.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:623:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:624:⸻
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:625:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:626:After Apollo
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:627:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:628:The period covered by these works—roughly from the late 1950s to the mid-1970s—has a particular coherence. It begins with uncertainty and ends with a sense of closure. The Moon landings mark both a culmination and a turning point. The narrative of ascent reaches its peak, and then, abruptly, it stalls.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:629:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:630:What follows is less often framed in religious terms. The language of destiny persists, particularly in discussions of Mars and beyond, but it is attenuated. The sense of collective purpose that characterised the early space race is harder to sustain. The frontier remains, but its moral clarity is diminished.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:631:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:632:This shift raises a question that these works collectively invite but do not fully answer: what happens to the religious imagination of spaceflight when the initial moment of transcendence has passed? If the Moon landing represents a kind of fulfilment, what follows is a period of recalibration. The categories that once structured the narrative—sacred and profane, destiny and restraint—are still available, but they no longer map as neatly onto the experience of space exploration.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:633:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:634:In this sense, the early space program can be understood as a bounded episode: a moment in which technological capability, political urgency, and religious imagination aligned in a particular way. The challenge, for subsequent generations, is to interpret that moment without collapsing its complexity into a single narrative.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:635:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:636:⸻
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:637:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:638:Conclusion
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:639:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:640:Taken together, these works resist the reduction of the American space program to either triumphalism or critique. They show that spaceflight was, and remains, a site of interpretive contestation. It invites readings that emphasise transcendence and those that foreground limitation. It can be framed as fulfilment of a destiny or as repetition of a pattern of overreach.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:641:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:642:The language of religion—of sacred and profane, of sin and redemption—provides one way of navigating this terrain. It does not resolve the tensions it names, but it makes them visible. The frontier myth, similarly, offers a narrative structure that both illuminates and obscures. It explains why spaceflight was compelling; it also risks importing assumptions that are not easily justified.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:643:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:644:First Man complicates both frameworks by attending to the experiential dimension of spaceflight—the silence, the isolation, the persistence of grief. It suggests that whatever meanings are projected onto space, they are mediated by the limits of human experience.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:645:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/generated ai personal essays.txt:646:The result is a more restrained, but also more precise, account of what the space program represents. Not a simple story of progress or hubris, but a moment in which a particular culture confronted the boundaries of its own imagination—and, in doing so, revealed both its aspirations and its constraints.

/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:2:  between sleep and grief. Personal but research-aware.

/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:4:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:5:Grief has its own clock. It does not respect the ordinary architecture of a day: work, meals, messages, errands, sleep. In the first weeks after a loss, night can stop being a place of rest and become a second room where the dead feel closer, the mind gets louder, and the body refuses the simple mercy of switching off.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:6:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:7:This is one of the crueller parts of grief. The person most in need of rest is often the least able to get it.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:8:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:9:Research supports what many grieving people already know in their bones: bereavement and sleep disturbance are closely linked. A systematic review found a high prevalence of sleep problems after bereavement, with stronger grief often associated with greater sleep difficulty. It also found higher rates of sleep disturbance among people experiencing complicated or prolonged grief, particularly where depression is also present.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:10:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:11:That matters because sleep is not just a pause from consciousness. It is an active biological process that affects memory, emotional regulation, attention, immune function and the body’s capacity to recover.   When grief disrupts sleep, it does not simply make us tired. It can change the texture of grief itself. Everything becomes harder to metabolise. Small decisions feel punitive. Social contact becomes more effortful. The ordinary world can seem too bright, too fast, too indifferent.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:12:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:13:After a death, insomnia is not always dramatic. Sometimes it is a clean, brutal wakefulness at 3 am. Sometimes it is the inability to fall asleep because the mind keeps replaying the last conversation, the hospital room, the phone call, the thing said badly, the thing never said. Sometimes sleep comes easily but breaks apart before dawn. Sometimes the body sleeps for ten hours and still wakes exhausted.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:14:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:15:There is a particular loneliness to being awake while everyone else is sleeping. During the day, grief may be held in place by tasks. There are forms to complete, people to update, meals to make, children to collect, work to return to too soon. At night, the scaffolding comes down. The mind has no meeting to attend, no polite sentence to finish. It begins its inventory.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:16:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:17:This is why advice about “good sleep habits” can land badly with grieving people. A fixed bedtime, less caffeine, a dark room and no phone in bed are useful. Most adults need around 7 to 9 hours of sleep each night, and regular routines can support sleep quality.   But grief is not poor time management. It is not caused by bad lighting or an overused screen. A person who has lost someone may already know exactly what sleep hygiene requires and still be unable to obey it.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:18:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:19:The better question is not “How do I force myself to sleep?” but “How do I make the night less hostile?”
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:20:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:21:That might mean lowering the stakes. If sleep does not come, rest still counts. Lying in the dark with one hand on the chest, listening to something familiar, moving to the couch for a while, or making tea without turning the kitchen into daylight can help the body remain in a quieter state. The goal is not to win against grief. It is to reduce the secondary panic that says, “Now I am grieving and failing at sleep.”
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:22:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:23:There is also a difference between remembering and ruminating, though they can feel identical at night. Remembering may hurt, but it has movement in it: a face, a phrase, a scene, a tenderness. Rumination loops. It prosecutes. It asks the same unanswerable question until the body becomes alert again. Writing can help, not because it solves anything, but because it gives the mind somewhere to put the loop. A notebook beside the bed can become a holding place for the thought that insists it must be dealt with at 2.17 am.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:24:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:25:Dreams add another layer. Grieving people often report dreams in which the dead return with startling ordinariness. They are in the kitchen. They are wearing the old jumper. They have not died after all. Waking from these dreams can feel like losing the person again before breakfast. A dream can be comforting, but it can also make the day harder. Wellness culture often wants to interpret every dream as healing. Some dreams are not healing. Some are just the sleeping mind trying to process an impossible absence.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:26:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:27:The research field is still developing. One review noted that, despite the obvious connection between bereavement and sleep, the literature has been relatively limited compared with related areas such as depression and trauma.   That limitation is important. It means we should be careful about neat claims. Poor sleep may intensify grief, and intense grief may disrupt sleep. The relationship can move in both directions. Recent work continues to examine this bidirectional pattern, especially in prolonged grief disorder.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:28:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:29:For some people, sleep gradually returns as the nervous system begins to trust the world again. The first full night can feel almost disloyal. There is a strange guilt in waking rested. Grief can make rest feel like abandonment, as if vigilance were a form of love. But exhaustion is not devotion. Staying awake does not keep the person closer. It only keeps the body in distress.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:30:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:31:For others, sleep problems persist and become their own condition. That is when support matters. Acute grief can include sadness, tearfulness and insomnia, and it may not require clinical treatment. But prolonged grief disorder describes grief that remains intense and disabling over time, preventing a person from adapting to the loss.   Persistent insomnia, especially when paired with depression, anxiety, intrusive thoughts or inability to function, deserves professional attention. This is not weakness. It is a health issue sitting inside a human loss.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:32:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:33:The most compassionate approach to sleep in grief is therefore practical but not simplistic. Keep the room cool and dark. Limit alcohol, which can make sleep more fragmented even if it initially makes you drowsy. Be careful with late caffeine. Use a routine, not as a command, but as a signal to the body.   Let mornings carry some light and movement, even if both feel offensive at first. Tell someone if nights are becoming frightening.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:34:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:35:And be wary of anyone who turns sleep into a moral achievement. Grief already gives people enough to feel guilty about. The aim is not perfect sleep. The aim is enough safety for the body to stand down, even briefly.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:36:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:37:Over time, sleep can become one of the ways grief changes shape. Not disappears. Not resolves neatly. But changes. The bed becomes less like a witness stand. The dark becomes less accusatory. The first thought on waking is not always the loss. Some mornings, there is a second or two before memory returns. Later, there may be mornings when memory returns gently.

/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:47:I was twenty-four when I realised my parents were not perfect. Not in a dramatic way. There was no slammed door, no revelation pulled from a drawer, no confession delivered across a kitchen table under a bad light. It happened in the ordinary weather of a Tuesday evening, while my father stood in the driveway holding a garden hose that had split near the nozzle, and my mother sat inside at the dining table, crying over an electricity bill.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:48:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:49:The house looked exactly as it always had. The same brick path. The same agapanthus along the fence. The same bowl by the front door where keys and coins and old receipts collected in a minor domestic archaeology. Everything was intact, which is perhaps why the moment unsettled me so deeply. The setting had not changed. Only my sight had.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:50:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:51:I had come home for dinner because I was tired and underfed and pretending not to be. My twenties had begun with the false confidence of someone who mistakes motion for direction. I had a job with an email signature, a rent payment that frightened me every fortnight, and a set of opinions about how life should be managed. I was old enough to see my parents’ habits as limitations, but not yet old enough to understand the labour that had made my own certainty possible.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:52:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:53:Until then, I had carried an image of them that was almost architectural. They were the frame around everything. My father knew where the torch was, how to reverse a trailer, what to do when the hot water went cold. My mother remembered birthdays, medical histories, the names of teachers I had not thought about in years. Between them, they seemed to possess the entire operating manual for being alive.

He stared at it for a second, as if it had betrayed him personally.

Her face had lost the composure I associated with her. She looked like someone who had reached the end of a calculation and found there was still more to pay.

/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:76:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:77:For years I had mistaken my parents’ strengths for completeness. My father’s competence had hidden his terror of helplessness. My mother’s care had hidden her resentment at having to notice everything. I had thought their flaws were irritations, things they could fix if they tried harder: his stubbornness, her worrying, his silence, her sharpness when tired. But sitting at that dining table, with the bill between them and water still dripping from my father’s trouser hem, I saw something more difficult. Their flaws were not decorative faults on otherwise finished people. They were part of the machinery. They were adaptations, old defences, compromises made so long ago they had become character.

/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:89:Children exempt their parents from personhood because they need them to be weatherproof. Even when we rebel against them, we often rebel against monuments. We accuse them of being controlling, embarrassing, old-fashioned, unreasonable, without fully understanding that these are not fixed properties. They are behaviours emerging from fear, fatigue, history, class, disappointment, biology, marriage, money, work, and the thousand small humiliations of adult life.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:90:

/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:92:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:93:After that night, memories rearranged themselves. My father sitting in the car after work before coming inside was no longer simply odd. It was a man taking three minutes of silence because the house would need him the second he opened the door. My mother checking the oven twice before we left for holidays was not just anxiety. It was the burden of being blamed, or blaming herself, if something went wrong. Their caution, their rules, their occasional emotional illiteracy: all of it had roots.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:94:

Some wounds remain wounds even after you learn who held the knife and why their hand was shaking. But the moral geometry of my family changed. There were fewer clean lines. Less prosecution. More evidence.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:96:

My mother sometimes made herself indispensable and then resented being needed. They both misremembered events in ways that protected them. They both gave advice shaped more by fear than by truth. They both loved me imperfectly, which is to say they loved me with the instruments they had.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:98:

I could no longer collapse into the old fantasy that somewhere, behind the visible surface of ordinary life, my parents were still in charge. The adults were not elsewhere. There was no hidden committee. No one was coming with the complete manual.

Perhaps adulthood was not a threshold crossed by competent people, but a room everyone enters unfinished. You learn where the torch is. You forget the thing you promised to remember. You pay the bill late. You say the wrong thing. You mend what can be mended and carry what cannot.

/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:111:3 Write a 1200-word cultural essay arguing that Joan Didion's The Year of Magical
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:112:  Thinking deserves to be reread every decade. Use specific examples from the
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:113:  book. For an arts publication
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:114:Why The year of magical thinking should be reread every decade
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:115:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:116:Joan Didion’s The year of magical thinking is often described as a grief memoir, which is accurate but insufficient. It is also a book about evidence, memory, control, marriage, medical language, social performance, and the collapse of ordinary time. Its subject is the year after the sudden death of Didion’s husband, John Gregory Dunne, while their daughter, Quintana, was gravely ill. But its deeper achievement is that it changes shape as the reader ages. Read in one decade, it is a devastating account of bereavement. Read in another, it becomes a study of how fragile the structures of adult life really are. Later again, it reads as an anatomy of denial, a critique of competence, and a warning about the stories we tell ourselves to survive.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:117:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:118:That is why it deserves to be reread every decade. Not because its meaning changes, exactly, but because the reader’s defences do.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:119:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:120:The book begins with a sentence that now feels almost proverbial: life changes fast. Didion does not stage grief as an emotional revelation. She stages it as administrative shock. One evening in December 2003, after visiting Quintana in hospital, Didion and Dunne return to their New York apartment. They sit down to dinner. He has a drink. He says something ordinary. Then he is dead. The horror of the scene lies in its lack of drama. There is no warning, no moral preparation, no cinematic pause. Didion’s account is precise because precision is the only instrument she still trusts.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:121:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:122:That precision matters. She remembers the fire, the table, the salad, the ambulance, the hospital, the paperwork. She reconstructs the event not only as a grieving widow but as a reporter. She seeks the timeline. She wants the medical explanation. She reads the records. She returns to the details because details imply sequence, and sequence implies cause. Cause, in turn, suggests that reality might still be governed by rules.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:123:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:124:This is one reason the book rewards rereading. In early adulthood, Didion’s forensic attention can look like strength. She knows what to do. She calls the right people. She signs forms. She keeps functioning. A decade later, the same composure reads differently. It becomes recognisable as shock, even as a kind of magical thinking in itself: the belief that if events can be accurately ordered, they might somehow become less final.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:125:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:126:The title’s “magical thinking” is not ornamental. It names the irrational logic by which grief tries to reverse the irreversible. Didion knows John is dead. She has seen the body. She has received the medical facts. Yet she cannot give away his shoes because he will need them when he returns. This is the book’s most famous example because it is so small and so devastating. It is not an extravagant delusion. It is domestic, practical, almost reasonable. A living man needs shoes. The mind accepts death abstractly while refusing its implications at the level of household management.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:127:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:128:That scene lands differently across a life. At twenty-five, it may seem like an elegant symbol. At thirty-five or forty-five, after enough losses, it reads less like symbolism than documentary fact. Grief does not always announce itself as despair. It can appear as a logistical hesitation: keep the shoes, preserve the room, do not cancel the account, do not move the glass. The dead remain embedded in systems designed for the living.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:129:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:130:Didion’s great cultural importance lies partly in her refusal to sentimentalise this. She does not turn grief into moral improvement. She does not claim loss made her wiser, kinder, or more authentic. The book is bracing because it rejects consolation as a literary obligation. She is not interested in uplift. She is interested in what the mind does under pressure.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:131:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:132:That refusal feels more valuable now, in a culture saturated with therapeutic scripts. Contemporary public grief often arrives already shaped for display: tribute posts, anniversary captions, curated vulnerability, the language of healing and closure. Didion offers something more austere and more useful. She shows grief as repetitive, self-interrupting, intellectually humiliating. She circles the same dates, the same medical facts, the same memories. She catches herself bargaining with reality. She notices how often she searches for the mistake in the system, the overlooked sign, the moment at which things might have gone otherwise.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:133:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:134:The “vortex effect” is central to this. Certain memories pull her out of the present and into an earlier life with John: Los Angeles, Malibu, Honolulu, working routines, family rituals, professional habits, private jokes. These are not decorative flashbacks. They show how marriage creates a shared archive so extensive that almost anything can trigger it. A place is not just a place. A hotel, a road, a hospital, a line from a novel, a weather pattern can become an opening into the lost life.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:135:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:136:Reread every decade, this aspect of the book becomes more consequential. The first time, the reader may focus on the death. Later, the book’s real subject may appear to be marriage: not romance as an event, but intimacy as accumulated reference. Didion and Dunne’s marriage was also a working partnership, an editorial relationship, a long conversation. His absence is therefore not only emotional. It is cognitive. The person who helped her verify reality is gone.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:137:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:138:That is one of the book’s sharpest insights. Grief is not only missing someone. It is losing a witness. John had been present for the formation of Didion’s adult life. Without him, her memories become unstable, not because they are false but because the person who could confirm their texture has disappeared. This is why the book is so exacting about memory. Didion does not trust it, but she cannot stop depending on it.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:139:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:140:The book also deserves periodic rereading because it changes alongside our relationship to institutions. Hospitals occupy much of its emotional terrain. Quintana’s illness means Didion is navigating intensive care, diagnoses, specialist language, medical uncertainty and bureaucratic routines at the same time as she is absorbing John’s death. She learns the language of sepsis, pneumonia, intracranial pressure, induced coma. She becomes fluent in the vocabulary of crisis because fluency offers a thin form of control.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:141:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:142:The medical scenes are not merely background. They expose the limits of expertise. Doctors know things Didion does not know, but they do not know everything she needs to know. They can explain mechanisms, not meanings. They can describe risk, not guarantee outcome. For readers who have spent more time with hospitals, ageing parents, chronic illness, disability systems or end-of-life care, these sections become more disturbing with each decade. The book understands that institutional language can be both necessary and unbearable. It gives names to events while failing to make them liveable.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:143:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:144:Didion’s style is inseparable from this argument. Her sentences are clean but not simple. The repetitions, returns and abrupt transitions enact the experience they describe. She writes as someone trying to create order while documenting the failure of order. The result is a prose style that feels controlled and destabilised at once. That tension is why the book still matters to cultural readers, not only to readers seeking a book about grief. It is a formal achievement: a work in which structure becomes evidence.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:145:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:146:There is also a harder reason to reread it. Each decade brings the reader closer to the book’s facts. Parents age. Partners become mortal in less theoretical ways. Friends die. Children become vulnerable adults. The body becomes less obedient. The systems that once seemed peripheral become central: hospitals, wills, emergency contacts, death certificates, intensive care units, insurance, memory itself. Didion’s book waits for the reader at each threshold.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:147:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:148:It is tempting to call The year of magical thinking timeless, but that word can flatten it. The book is intensely situated: in New York, in a particular marriage, in a particular class world, in the medical and literary culture of the early 2000s. Its durability comes not from universality in the abstract, but from the accuracy with which Didion records a specific consciousness under extreme strain. She does not speak for everyone. She speaks exactly enough that others can recognise themselves.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:149:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:150:To reread the book every decade is to submit to a test: what did you miss last time because you had not yet needed to know it? At one age, the unforgettable image is the shoes. At another, it is the dinner table. Later, it may be the hospital corridor, the medical record, the sudden unreliability of memory, or the terrible fact that ordinary life continues to look ordinary until it does not.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:151:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:152:Didion’s achievement is not that she makes grief intelligible. She shows why it resists intelligence. She shows the mind attempting to negotiate with death through syntax, research, manners, memory and routine. She shows the bargain failing. Then she keeps looking.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:153:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:154:That is why the book should not be left on the shelf as a classic once read and respectfully admired. It should be returned to, not for comfort, but for calibration. Every decade, it will measure something different: what has been lost, what is now feared, what can no longer be controlled, and what forms of magical thinking still remain.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:155:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:156:4 Write a 1300-word reflective piece for a tech blog about why I've stopped using
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:157:  productivity apps. Personal, slightly contrarian, with practical observations.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:158:Why I stopped using productivity apps
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:159:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:160:I used to believe the right productivity app would eventually fix the problem.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:161:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:162:Not the obvious problem, which was that I had too much to do. The subtler problem: that I did not have a clean enough system for holding it all. Tasks lived in too many places. Notes were scattered. Ideas arrived while walking, showering, driving, scrolling, sitting in meetings, half-listening to myself promise something I knew I would forget unless I captured it immediately.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:163:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:164:So I tried apps.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:165:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:166:Task managers. Notes apps. Whiteboards. Kanban boards. Habit trackers. Calendar overlays. Focus timers. Personal knowledge management tools. Read-it-later apps. Browser extensions. Automations. Dashboards. Apps that promised simplicity. Apps that promised depth. Apps that promised to become the second brain I apparently needed because the first one was being throttled by work, life, tabs, notifications and vague dread.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:167:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:168:For a while, each new tool worked. That is the trick of productivity software: the first week always feels like progress. You are not yet maintaining the system. You are imagining the person you will become once the system is complete.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:169:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:170:You make categories. You tag things. You build a dashboard. You watch someone on YouTube explain their frictionless weekly review and think, yes, this is the missing layer. The app becomes a little model of a better self: calmer, more intentional, less reactive, never surprised by a deadline, never leaving a receipt in a tote bag or a document in the wrong folder.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:171:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:172:Then reality resumes.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:173:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:174:The task manager fills with stale intentions. The notes app becomes a storage unit for things you were once excited to think about. The habit tracker quietly records your failure until you stop opening it. The dashboard becomes another surface to check. The automation breaks. The tags proliferate. The system that was meant to reduce cognitive load becomes a small administrative job with no salary and no end date.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:175:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:176:I did not stop using productivity apps because they are useless. Some are very good. I stopped because I finally admitted that I was using them to avoid a more uncomfortable question: what actually deserves my attention?
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:177:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:178:Most productivity apps are built around capture. Capture the task. Capture the idea. Capture the meeting note. Capture the article. Capture the insight. Capture the thing someone mentioned in passing that might matter later. Capture everything, because storage is cheap and forgetting feels irresponsible.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:179:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:180:But capture is not the same as commitment.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:181:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:182:A captured task still needs a decision. A saved article still needs time. A note still needs synthesis. A list still needs priority. Every item preserved for later becomes a tiny claim on the future. The app does not remove that claim. It just makes it look orderly.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:183:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:184:That was the first practical observation: I was not overwhelmed because I lacked a reliable capture system. I was overwhelmed because I was capturing too much and deciding too little.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:185:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:186:There is a particular relief in writing something down. It feels like moving it out of the mind. But if the thing matters, it returns. If it does not return, perhaps it did not matter enough. I do not mean this as a universal rule. Some work genuinely requires rigorous tracking. But in my own life, I had confused documentation with discernment. I kept preserving things to avoid having to choose.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:187:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:188:The second observation was that productivity apps reward tidying, not doing.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:189:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:190:This is not a moral failing. It is a design pattern. Apps have interfaces. Interfaces invite interaction. You can drag, sort, nest, colour, schedule, reschedule, link, archive, review and reframe. These actions feel constructive because they are concrete. They produce visible change. The list is cleaner. The board is aligned. The note has metadata. The project has a template.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:191:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:192:Meanwhile, the hard thing remains untouched.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:193:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:194:Writing the difficult email. Reading the dense document. Making the decision. Having the conversation. Starting the draft. Closing the loop.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:195:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:196:Productivity theatre is still theatre even when the stage is beautifully designed.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:197:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:198:The more elaborate my system became, the easier it was to spend the first hour of a workday preparing to work. I was not procrastinating in the usual sense. I was doing something adjacent to useful. That made it harder to challenge. It looked disciplined from the outside and felt responsible from the inside. But the practical outcome was the same: I had spent energy managing representations of work instead of doing the work itself.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:199:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:200:The third observation was that most apps assume the future is more stable than it is.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:201:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:202:A task entered on Monday can feel precise: draft proposal, review deck, call supplier, write brief. By Thursday, the context has changed. The meeting moved. Someone sent new information. The urgent thing became irrelevant. The small thing became political. The neat sequence collapsed.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:203:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:204:Productivity apps tend to treat this as a planning issue. Build better workflows. Add dependencies. Use recurring reviews. Reprioritise.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:205:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:206:Sometimes that helps. But often the problem is not that the plan needs maintenance. The problem is that the plan was a fiction. Useful, perhaps, but still a fiction.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:207:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:208:A weekly plan can create orientation. It cannot eliminate volatility. When I tried to force volatile work into rigid systems, I generated more maintenance than clarity. My app was always asking me to reconcile yesterday’s assumptions with today’s conditions. Eventually, that reconciliation became its own workload.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:209:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:210:The fourth observation was more personal: productivity apps made me more attentive to failure than momentum.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:211:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:212:A completed task disappears. An overdue task glows.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:213:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:214:This is rational from a software perspective. Systems need to show what still requires action. But psychologically, it meant I spent a lot of time looking at evidence of what I had not done. Even a productive day ended with the app reminding me of the residue. The list was never empty because the list was not a measure of effort. It was a measure of everything that could be imagined, requested, deferred or worried about.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:215:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:216:That distinction matters.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:217:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:218:A to-do list can be endless because possibility is endless. Capacity is not. Treating the list as the source of truth gives the list too much authority. It starts to feel as though the problem is personal inefficiency rather than structural excess.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:219:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:220:This is where my view has become slightly contrarian: I think many productivity systems individualise what are often workload, priority and boundary problems.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:221:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:222:If work is badly scoped, a better task manager will not fix it. If a team has no clear decision rights, a better notes system will not fix it. If every channel is treated as urgent, a focus app will not fix it. If you are trying to do too many things because saying no has social or professional costs, a habit tracker will not save you.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:223:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:224:The app may help you survive the chaos. It may not help you question it.

/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:226:That is not a reason to reject tools altogether. It is a reason to stop asking tools to solve organisational and human problems they can only display.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:227:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:228:Since stepping away from productivity apps, my system is less impressive and more honest.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:229:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:230:I use a calendar for commitments that involve time or other people. I keep a short written list for the day or week. I keep notes where they are useful, not where they satisfy an architecture. I do not try to capture every idea. I do not build dashboards for my life. I do not track habits unless there is a specific reason. I let some things be forgotten.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:231:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:232:The last part is harder than it sounds.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:233:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:234:There is a small arrogance in believing every thought should be preserved. There is also fear. Fear that forgetting means losing value. Fear that an uncaptured idea will prove important later. Fear that an untracked task will expose you as careless.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:235:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:236:But memory has its own filtering function. So does recurrence. If something matters, it often reappears through consequence, conversation, discomfort or need. Not always. But often enough that I no longer treat total capture as a virtue.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:237:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:238:My current approach is deliberately plain.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:239:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:240:Each morning, I ask what would make the day meaningfully complete. Not ideal. Not maximally productive. Complete. Usually the answer is one to three things. Sometimes it is one difficult thing and several minor obligations. Sometimes it is simply staying ahead of a deadline without making the rest of the week worse.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:241:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:242:I write those things down.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:243:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:244:If new tasks arrive, I decide whether they belong today, later or nowhere. “Later” is not a magic category anymore. It has to earn its place. If I cannot imagine when I would do it, or why it matters, I try not to store it as a moral debt.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:245:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:246:For reading and ideas, I have become more ruthless. I save less. I finish less. I trust skimming more. Not every useful article needs to become a note. Not every note needs to become an insight. Not every insight needs to be operationalised.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:247:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:248:This has changed how I think about attention. I no longer see it as something to optimise through tooling. I see it as something to spend.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:249:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:250:That framing is less glamorous, but more practical. Spending attention implies trade-offs. It makes waste visible. It also makes rest legitimate. You cannot spend continuously without depletion. No app can negotiate that physics on your behalf.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:251:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:252:The irony is that stopping productivity apps has probably made me more productive, but not in the quantified, dashboard-friendly sense. I finish more important things because fewer trivial things get promoted into the system. I feel less compelled to maintain a perfect inventory of obligations. I notice sooner when the issue is not organisation but overcommitment. I have fewer places to look and fewer ways to pretend I am working.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:253:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:254:The cost is that the system is not elegant. It does not scale infinitely. It does not produce satisfying analytics. It depends on judgement, which is inconvenient and cannot be fully automated.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:255:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:256:But that is also the point.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:257:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:258:A good tool should support judgement, not replace it. My problem was that I kept outsourcing judgement to systems that could only organise inputs. They could tell me what I had entered, tagged, dated and deferred. They could not tell me what mattered, what to abandon, what to renegotiate, or what kind of day I was actually capable of having.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:259:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:260:So I stopped looking for the app that would make me a better version of myself.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:261:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:262:I needed less infrastructure and more refusal. Less capture and more choice. Fewer lists and a clearer sense of what did not belong on them.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/examples ai.txt:263:
