/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:35:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:36:## Em dashes
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:37:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:38:In publication-ready plain prose, em dashes are now a strong AI signal. Density is the giveaway: a writer using one or two intentionally is fine; a piece with em dashes in every other paragraph reads as machine-flavoured even when each individual use is grammatical.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:39:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:40:Most em dashes are doing the job of a different punctuation mark. The right replacement depends on what the dash was actually doing.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:41:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:42:| AI tell | What it usually means | Notes |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:43:|---|---|---|
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:44:| Em dash between two independent clauses | A period or semicolon | Period if both halves carry weight. Semicolon if the two clauses are independent and the relationship is real. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:45:| Em dash setting off an aside | A comma or parentheses | Parentheses if the aside could be cut without losing the sentence. Comma if it could not. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:46:| Em dash introducing a list or explanation | A colon | "X, and here is what X means" is colon work. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:47:| Em dash separating a beat or shift | A comma | The most common case. Try the comma first. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:48:| Em dash that fits none of the above | A sentence with bad structure | Rewrite. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:49:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:50:---
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:51:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:52:## AI vocabulary words
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:53:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:54:Words that appear far more frequently in post-2023 LLM output than in human writing. No single word is proof. The tell is density: three or more in one paragraph reads as AI even when each word is individually defensible.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:55:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:56:The vocabulary clusters by semantic function: verbs of intellectual work, inflation verbs, action-flavoured corporate verbs, and abstract scenery nouns. The middle column names what the AI word usually stands in for. The writer picks the meaning that fits and uses that word, or cuts the verb if none of the meanings apply.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:57:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:58:For long-tail vocabulary (the Kobak 900-word list, the GPTZero 100-phrase list), Suggestions does not look up individual entries here. It uses the cluster guidance below to draft a rewrite appropriate to the flagged occurrence.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:59:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:60:### Verbs of intellectual work
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:61:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:62:| AI word | What it usually means | Notes |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:63:|---|---|---|
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:64:| delve / delve into | analyse, investigate, research, study, get into the detail | Pick the verb that names what you are actually doing. If none fit, the sentence does not need the verb. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:65:| explore | try, test, investigate, look at possibilities | Fine when the genuine meaning is *consider possibilities*. AI-flavoured when it stands in for any verb of inquiry. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:66:| examine | check, read, study, test | Often the sentence works without it. "Examine the data" usually wants to be "the data shows…". |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:67:| navigate (figurative) | manage, handle, work out | Drop entirely when the object is concrete. "Navigate the system" wants to be "use the system". |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:68:| unpack | explain, work out, take apart | Now AI-flavoured despite colloquial origin. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:69:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:70:### Inflation verbs
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:71:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:72:These words promise analytical work the sentence does not do. The plain alternatives are usually shorter and more honest.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:73:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:74:| AI word | What it usually means | Notes |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:75:|---|---|---|
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:76:| underscore(s) | shows, says, repeats | If the sentence already shows the thing, cut the verb entirely. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:77:| highlight(s) | shows, picks out, names | A sign the writer has not done the work of saying *what* it shows. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:78:| emphasise(s) | stresses, repeats, focuses on | Often empty when the next clause is already doing the emphasis. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:79:| illustrate(s) | shows, gives an example of | The next sentence should be the example. The verb is doing nothing. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:80:| reflect(s) (figurative) | shows, comes from, follows from | Drop entirely when the connection is already explicit. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:81:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:82:### Action-flavoured corporate verbs
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:83:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:84:| AI word | What it usually means | Notes |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:85:|---|---|---|
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:86:| leverage | use, apply, draw on | "Use" is almost always right. "Leverage" carries no extra information. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:87:| harness | use, take advantage of | Almost always means "use". |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:88:| foster | encourage, build, support | If the subject cannot actually foster (an abstraction "fostering" something), rewrite. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:89:| facilitate | run, host, lead, let, help | Almost always means "do" or "let happen". Say which. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:90:| enable | let, allow | Fine occasionally. Flag when it stacks with other corporate verbs. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:91:| streamline | simplify, cut, shorten, speed up | Name what was cut. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:92:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:93:### Abstract scenery nouns
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:94:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:95:| AI word | What it usually means | Notes |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:96:|---|---|---|
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:97:| landscape (figurative) | the field, the topic, the situation, or just cut | "The funding landscape" wants to be "funding". "The political landscape" wants to be "politics". |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:98:| tapestry | a mix, a list of named things | Almost never lands. Replace with a concrete plural such as "traditions" or "histories". |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:99:| realm | the field, the area, or just cut | "The realm of X" wants to be "X". |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:100:| interplay | how X and Y work together | Fine in academic writing about specific dynamics. AI-flavoured when it stands in for "the thing". |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:101:| mosaic of / tapestry of | mix of, list of, range of | Often a wrapper around a plural noun. Drop it. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:102:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:103:### Cluster-level fix
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:104:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:105:When a paragraph trips the AI-vocabulary density check (three or more in one paragraph), single-word substitution rarely fixes it. Rewrite the paragraph around its concrete claim, with concrete verbs in place of the abstract ones and no scenery nouns.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:106:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:107:---
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:108:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:109:## Significance inflation
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:110:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:111:Constructions that claim symbolic weight for ordinary things: "stands as a testament", "marks a pivotal moment", "plays a crucial role", "represents a shift". The construction promises significance without supplying evidence.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:112:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:113:| AI tell | What it usually means | Notes |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:114:|---|---|---|
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:115:| stands as a testament to | shows, is evidence for, proves | Often the sentence already says it. Cut the construction. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:116:| marks a pivotal moment in | started, changed, was the first | Pick the verb that names what actually happened. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:117:| plays a crucial role in | matters for, is part of, helps | Cut "crucial role" and name the role. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:118:| represents a (shift / turning point) | started, changed, ended | The construction promises drama. Just say what changed. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:119:| reflects (broader / wider / deeper) | comes from, is part of, is one example of | Drop the inflation adjective; name the specific connection. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:120:| sets the stage for | started, made possible, came before | Almost always padding. Cut. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:121:| has left an indelible mark | shaped, changed, influenced | If the mark is real, it can be named. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:122:| paves the way for | leads to, comes before, makes possible | Cliché. Pick the plain verb. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:123:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:124:---
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:125:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:126:## Copula avoidance
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:127:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:128:Constructions that substitute elaborate verbs for "is", "are", or "has". Often the elaborate verb does nothing the simple copula could not.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:129:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:130:| AI tell | What it usually means | Notes |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:131:|---|---|---|
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:132:| serves as | is | Almost always. "X serves as the venue for Y" wants to be "X is the venue for Y". |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:133:| stands as | is | Same as serves as. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:134:| marks | is, was | "X marks the largest Y" wants to be "X is the largest Y". |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:135:| represents (literal) | is, shows | "X represents an attempt to Y" wants to be "X is an attempt to Y". |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:136:| boasts | has | "The town boasts three museums" wants to be "The town has three museums". |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:137:| features | has, includes | "The building features X" wants to be "The building has X". |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:138:| offers | has, gives | "The role offers flexibility" wants to be "The role has flexible hours" (be specific about what). |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:139:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:140:---
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:141:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:142:## Hedging clusters
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:143:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:144:Stacking three or more qualifiers on a single claim until the sentence commits to nothing. The fix is rarely to find a less hedgy word; it is to drop the hedges and let the claim stand or fall on its own.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:145:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:146:| AI tell | What it usually means | Notes |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:147:|---|---|---|
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:148:| could potentially possibly | might, could | Pick one qualifier or none. Three is meaningless. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:149:| It could be argued that | (just argue it) | If the writer thinks X, they should claim X. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:150:| It might be the case that | maybe, possibly | One word, not five. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:151:| seems to suggest | suggests, hints at | "Seems to" almost always cuts cleanly. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:152:| there is some evidence that may indicate | the evidence shows, the evidence is mixed | Either claim it or admit the evidence is mixed. The hedge stack disguises uncertainty as caution. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:153:| it is worth considering whether | (just consider it) | If it is worth considering, the writer can do the considering on the page. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:154:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:155:---
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:156:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:157:## Filler phrases
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:158:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:159:Multi-word constructions that compress to one or two words, or cut entirely. Filler is the easiest tell to remove because the meaning rarely depends on it.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:160:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:161:| AI tell | What it usually means | Notes |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:162:|---|---|---|
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:163:| in order to | to | Always. "In order to" never adds anything. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:164:| due to the fact that | because | Always. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:165:| at this point in time | now, today, currently | Pick the one that fits the tense. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:166:| in the event that | if | Always. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:167:| has the ability to | can | Always. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:168:| it is important to note that | (cut the whole phrase) | The next clause should make its own importance evident. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:169:| at the end of the day | (cut entirely; or state the actual conclusion) | Adds nothing. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:170:| from a broader perspective | (cut, or name the perspective) | Vague gesture. Either name the wider context or drop it. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:171:| generally / broadly speaking | usually, often, mostly | Or cut. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:172:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:173:---
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:174:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:175:## Formulaic openers
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:176:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:177:Standard AI-essay opening patterns that signal the piece was generated rather than written. The first sentence often gives the game away.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:178:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:179:| AI tell | What it usually means | Notes |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:180:|---|---|---|
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:181:| In today's fast-paced world | (cut entirely; start with the actual subject) | The world is not the subject of the piece. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:182:| As technology continues to evolve | (cut; start with the actual claim) | Time and progress are not arguments. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:183:| In an era of (X) | (cut; or name a specific year) | Vague historical framing. Pin to a date or remove. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:184:| In recent years | recently, since (year), in the past (n) years | If recent matters, pick a specific window. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:185:| Throughout history | (almost always cut) | Rarely true. The claim usually applies to a much narrower window. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:186:| It is no secret that | (cut entirely) | If everyone knows, the writer has not earned the sentence. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:187:| Many people believe / It is widely known | (name the people, or drop the claim) | Vague attribution. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:188:| Imagine a world where | (cut; just describe the situation) | Hypothetical-opener cliché. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:189:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:190:---
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:191:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:192:## Signposted-conclusion openers
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:193:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:194:Phrases that announce a conclusion is coming. They tell the reader what to feel rather than letting the conclusion arrive on its own merits.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:195:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:196:| AI tell | What it usually means | Notes |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:197:|---|---|---|
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:198:| In conclusion | (cut; let the final paragraph stand) | Reading the last paragraph is enough cue. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:199:| To sum up | (cut) | Same. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:200:| To summarise | (cut) | Same. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:201:| Ultimately | (cut, or claim the conclusion directly) | "Ultimately, X" usually works as just "X". |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:202:| In the end | (cut) | Same. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:203:| All things considered | (cut) | Performs balance the writer has not actually shown. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:204:| The takeaway is | (cut; just state the takeaway) | The reader does not need a label on it. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:205:| With that distinction in mind | (cut) | Filler transition. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:206:| Looking ahead | (cut; or state the specific prediction) | Vague forward-glance. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:207:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:208:---
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:209:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:210:## Generic positive conclusions
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:211:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:212:Vague upbeat endings that could be appended to any article. The fix is to replace with a specific fact, observation, or claim.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:213:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:214:| AI tell | What it usually means | Notes |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:215:|---|---|---|
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:216:| The future looks bright | (replace with a specific plan, statistic, or expectation) | Generic optimism. Name what specifically looks bright and why. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:217:| Exciting times lie ahead | (replace with a specific upcoming event or development) | Same. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:218:| This represents a step in the right direction | (claim the specific improvement) | "Right direction" is a placeholder. Say what improved and how. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:219:| The journey continues | (cut entirely, or name the next stage) | Empty rhetorical close. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:220:| Only time will tell | (replace with a specific question or test) | Hedged optimism. Name the unresolved question. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:221:| Continues to thrive | (state the specific evidence of thriving) | Empty without numbers or examples. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:222:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:223:---
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:224:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:225:## Soft scaffold phrases
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:226:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:227:Bland labelled-block introductions that mark a generated explainer arranging information into balanced sections. The phrases survive because they are not flashy, which is exactly the problem.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:228:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:229:| AI tell | What it usually means | Notes |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:230:|---|---|---|
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:231:| One useful area is | (cut; name the area directly) | "One useful area is product design" wants to be "Product design is one example". |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:232:| Another useful area is | (cut; name the area) | Same. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:233:| The main strength is | (claim the strength directly) | "The main strength is X" wants to be "X". |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:234:| The main risk is | (claim the risk) | Same. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:235:| Good use usually comes down to | (cut; state what good use looks like) | Vague gesture toward criteria. State them. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:236:| One thing to consider is | (cut; just consider it) | "One thing to consider is Y" wants to be "Consider Y" or just "Y". |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:237:| It is worth noting that | (cut; note it) | If it is worth noting, just note it. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:238:| What's interesting is | (cut; state what is interesting) | The writer is announcing rather than showing. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:239:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:240:---
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:241:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:242:## Manufactured-insight phrasings
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:243:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:244:Phrases that promise revelation without doing the evidentiary work. They include false revelation ("the real answer is"), contrived contrarianism ("what nobody is talking about"), and privileged perception ("when no one was watching").
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:245:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:246:| AI tell | What it usually means | Notes |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:247:|---|---|---|
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:248:| The real X is | (claim X directly without the "real" frame) | If the writer has the real answer, they can just state it. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:249:| Here's what's really happening | (state what is happening) | The "really" is doing nothing. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:250:| What nobody is talking about | (cite a specific source for the silence, or drop) | Almost always false. Many people talk about most things. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:251:| Contrary to popular belief | (name the belief and source it; or drop) | If the popular belief is real, name it specifically. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:252:| The uncomfortable truth | (state the truth without the framing) | The framing inflates ordinary claims. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:253:| What gets lost in the conversation | (state what is missing, with evidence) | Often manufactured. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:254:| When no one was watching | (cut; the writer probably was not there) | Privileged-perception cliché. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:255:| Without anyone noticing | (cut) | Same. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:256:| The shift nobody noticed | (cut, or name the shift and source the silence) | Same. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:257:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:258:---
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:259:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:260:## Sycophantic / collaborative artifacts
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:261:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:262:Chatbot turn-taking residue left in published prose. These come from the assistant register and have no business in finished writing.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:263:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:264:| AI tell | What it usually means | Notes |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:265:|---|---|---|
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:266:| Great question! | (cut entirely) | Never appropriate in published prose. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:267:| Of course! | (cut) | Same. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:268:| Certainly! | (cut) | Same. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:269:| You're absolutely right! | (cut) | Same. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:270:| I hope this helps! | (cut) | Same. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:271:| Let me know if you'd like me to expand | (cut) | Same. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:272:| Here is an overview of | (cut; just give the overview) | Meta-narration that points at the content instead of being it. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:273:| Would you like | (cut; or rephrase as a direct question to the reader if appropriate) | Chat register. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:274:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:275:---
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:276:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:277:## Knowledge-cutoff disclaimers
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:278:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:279:AI hedges about its own training data left in published text. The fix is either to find the actual information or to cut the disclaimer.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:280:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:281:| AI tell | What it usually means | Notes |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:282:|---|---|---|
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:283:| As of (date) | (cut, or replace with a specific date and source) | Often left over from prompts asking the model what it knows. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:284:| Up to my last training update | (cut) | Pure model artefact. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:285:| While specific details are (limited / scarce / not extensively documented) | (find the details, or rewrite the sentence to make a different claim) | If the writer cannot find the detail, the sentence should not pretend to. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:286:| Based on available information | (cut, or name the source) | Vague hedge. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:287:| It appears that | (claim it, or hedge with a specific reason) | "It appears that" with no follow-up evidence is empty. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:288:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:289:---
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:290:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:291:## Contrived contrast / negative parallelism
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:292:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:293:The "not just X, it's Y" construction and its variants. The problem is structural: a flat interpretation gets dismissed so an inflated abstraction can arrive with fake depth. Substitution does not fix this. The rewrite has to drop the contrast altogether and make a direct claim.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:294:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:295:The middle column names what the writer was *trying* to say. The Notes column says how to rewrite.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:296:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:297:| AI tell | What it usually means | Notes |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:298:|---|---|---|
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:299:| It's not X, it's Y | The writer wants to claim Y but has not supported it | Pick Y, support it with evidence, drop the rejection of X. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:300:| It's Y, not X | Same as above | Reversing the order does not change the construction. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:301:| Less X than Y | The writer wants to claim Y but has hedged with the comparison | "Less interesting than important" wants to be "important, because…". |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:302:| More Y than X | Same as Less X than Y | Same construction, opposite framing. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:303:| Not so much X as Y | Same as above | The "as much" softening adds nothing. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:304:| Beyond X, it is Y | The writer wants Y to be the subject | "Beyond grief, it becomes a meditation on belonging" wants to be "The film is about how belonging changes after a death." |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:305:| You might think X. Actually, Y | The writer wants to claim Y but has manufactured suspense | Just claim Y. The reader was not thinking X. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:306:| No X. No Y. Just Z | The writer wants to state Z dramatically | State Z directly. The negation series performs depth without earning it. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:307:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:308:### When the contrast is real
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:309:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:310:Not every contrast is contrived. A concrete distinction between two specific things ("the laptop is powerful, not cheap") is doing real work. Two named positions in genuine tension ("the problem is not collaboration, the problem is performative attendance") can carry an argument.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:311:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:312:The tell is the *inflated reveal*: the second half of the contrast is an abstract noun (meaning, identity, humanity, trust, belonging, connection) that the writer has not actually argued for. If the Y side is concrete and supported, the contrast is probably fine. If the Y side is an abstract payload, rewrite it as a direct claim.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:313:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:314:---
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:315:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:316:## False-concession constructions
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:317:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:318:Performing balance by staging two generic positions and landing in the middle. Real nuance names the evidence and stakes; false concession just balances abstractions.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:319:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:320:The middle column names what the writer was trying to do. Substitution does not work; the rewrite has to drop the staged debate and make a direct claim.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:321:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:322:| AI tell | What it usually means | Notes |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:323:|---|---|---|
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:324:| While critics argue X, supporters say Y | The writer wants to avoid taking a position | Pick a position, support it. The staged debate is performative. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:325:| The truth lies somewhere in the middle | The writer has not committed to a claim | Either name the actual middle position with evidence, or drop the framing and pick a side. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:326:| Both sides have valid points | Same as above | If both sides have valid points, name which points and on what evidence. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:327:| It depends on context | (when used to dodge) | Acceptable when the dependence is named. Empty when "it depends" is the whole answer. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:328:| There are merits and drawbacks to both | The writer is dodging | Same as above. List the actual merits and drawbacks. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:329:| On the one hand X, on the other hand Y | The writer is staging a debate without picking | Pick the side the evidence supports, or admit the question is genuinely open and name what would settle it. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:330:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:331:---
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:332:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:333:## Bland critical templates
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:334:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:335:Stock evaluation phrases used in book and film reviews. They sound critical but commit to nothing specific. Common in AI-generated reviews because they are common in the genre's training data.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:336:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:337:The middle column says what concrete claim the phrase usually substitutes for. The rewrite has to point at a specific scene, sentence, performance, or formal choice.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:338:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:339:| AI tell | What it usually means | Notes |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:340:|---|---|---|
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:341:| ambitious in an old-fashioned way | (a specific formal choice the reviewer should name) | What is ambitious about it, and which old-fashioned tradition does it follow? |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:342:| earns its weight | (a specific scene or argument that justifies the length) | Empty without an example. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:343:| social texture | (specific details about what people do, say, and own in the work) | Replace with the texture itself. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:344:| emotional range | (the specific emotions the work covers, with examples) | Name two or three of them. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:345:| field of sympathy | (which characters or positions the work treats with warmth) | Name them. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:346:| moral strength | (the specific stance the work takes, and on what) | Empty without a position. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:347:| slow revelation | (what specifically is revealed, and over what time) | Fine when the revelation is named. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:348:| quietly devastating | (the specific scene or line that does the work) | Cliché unless anchored. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:349:| richly observed | (the specific observations) | Same. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:350:| beautifully crafted | (the specific formal choices) | Same. |
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:351:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/alternatives.md:352:---
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:1:# Human-eyes Rewrite and Write Process
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:2:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:3:This file is required for Rewrite and Write actions. Use it as the operating
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:4:procedure after the audit has identified patterns and before returning prose.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:5:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:6:## Step A: Structural Pass
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:7:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:8:Do this before changing surface wording.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:9:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:10:1. Identify the current paragraph rhythm.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:11:   - Count paragraphs.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:12:   - Notice whether most paragraphs are similar length.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:13:   - Notice whether paragraphs end with tidy summaries.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:14:   - Notice whether sections follow the same arc: setup, explanation, moral.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:15:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:16:2. Break repeated arcs.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:17:   - Turn one explanatory paragraph into a short direct paragraph.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:18:   - Merge two thin paragraphs when they repeat the same movement.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:19:   - Split a long paragraph when it contains a turn, example, or qualification.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:20:   - Let some paragraphs end on concrete detail rather than a summary sentence.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:21:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:22:3. Vary sentence rhythm.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:23:   - Keep some short sentences if they carry stance or timing.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:24:   - Add longer sentences only when they carry specific detail, not padding.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:25:   - Avoid several paragraphs with the same medium-length explanatory cadence.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:26:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:27:4. Reduce list cadence.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:28:   - Cut decorative triads.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:29:   - Break long inventories into prose when the list is only rhythm.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:30:   - Preserve lists when accumulation is the point, but say so in the self-check.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:31:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:32:5. Remove reframe laundering.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:33:   - Replace "not X but Y", "less X than Y", "beyond X", and similar reveal
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:34:     structures with direct claims.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:35:   - Keep ordinary factual contrast when it is needed for meaning.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:36:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:37:## Step B: Surface Pass
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:38:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:39:After structure is fixed, remove required surface tells for the chosen depth.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:40:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:41:Balanced must fix hard failures and strong warnings:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:42:- assistant residue
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:43:- generic conclusions
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:44:- placeholder residue
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:45:- manufactured insight
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:46:- false concession hedges
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:47:- formulaic openers
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:48:- signposted conclusions
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:49:- contrived contrast
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:50:- AI vocabulary clusters
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:51:- soft scaffolding
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:52:- excessive hedging
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:53:- em dashes unless preserving them is explicitly justified by voice
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:54:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:55:All must also fix context warnings unless meaning would materially change.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:56:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:57:Use `references/alternatives.md` for lexical substitutions when the issue is a
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:58:word or stock phrase. Do not use substitutions for structural problems; rewrite
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:59:the sentence or paragraph instead.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:60:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:61:## Step C: Structural Self-Check
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:62:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:63:Before returning, answer the eight self-check fields in the output template.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:64:Be concrete. Do not claim a pattern was fixed unless the draft changed in a way
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:65:the reader can see.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:66:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:67:1. Section arcs: count how many paragraphs or sections still follow the same arc.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:68:2. Resolution density: count tidy summary endings that remain.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:69:3. Register breaks: name at least one place where rhythm, register, or sentence
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:70:   shape breaks the default smoothness.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:71:4. Triads: count obvious three-part lists and state what was cut or preserved.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:72:5. Reframe laundering: state whether contrived contrast remains.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:73:6. Purposeful devices preserved: list devices kept because they serve meaning.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:74:7. Stance: state whether the rewrite preserved, weakened, or strengthened stance.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:75:8. Remaining tells: list remaining failed checks, or say none.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:76:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:77:## Step D: Semantic Preservation
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:78:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:79:Compare the rewrite or draft against the brief or source before returning.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:80:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:81:For Rewrite:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:82:- Preserve the source argument, examples, stance, and genre.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:83:- Preserve factual claims unless the original claim is unclear or unsupported.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:84:- Do not make a writer more neutral just to sound clean.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:85:- Do not remove a distinctive device if removing it would flatten the voice;
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:86:  disclose the preservation instead.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:87:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:88:For Write:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:89:- Answer the brief directly.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:90:- Include concrete sensory, practical, or argumentative detail.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:91:- Avoid generic essay structure unless the user asked for it.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:92:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:93:## Step E: Re-Grade and Revise
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:94:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:95:Run the grader or equivalent check after the final draft.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:96:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:97:If the chosen depth has required failures:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:98:1. Revise the draft before returning.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:99:2. Re-grade after the revision.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:100:3. Repeat up to two revision passes.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:101:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:102:Stop with residual issues only when:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:103:- the issue is deliberately preserved for voice or meaning, or
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:104:- removing it would materially change the source, or
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:105:- two revision passes have failed to clear it.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:106:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:107:When residual issues remain, name them plainly in the post-check. Do not say
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/process.md:108:"none identified" if the grader still reports required failures.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:1:# Worked example: AI-sounding draft → human-sounding rewrite
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:2:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:3:End-to-end demonstration of a rewrite at All depth: an AI-generated essay through audit, rewrite, structural self-check, and revised rewrite. Use as a reference for what a real Rewrite action's output should look like.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:4:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:5:## Before (AI-sounding)
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:6:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:7:> Great question! Here is an essay on this topic. I hope this helps!
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:8:>
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:9:> AI-assisted coding serves as an enduring testament to the transformative potential of large language models, marking a pivotal moment in the evolution of software development. In today's rapidly evolving technological landscape, these groundbreaking tools--nestled at the intersection of research and practice--are reshaping how engineers ideate, iterate, and deliver, underscoring their vital role in modern workflows.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:10:>
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:11:> At its core, the value proposition is clear: streamlining processes, enhancing collaboration, and fostering alignment. It's not just about autocomplete; it's about unlocking creativity at scale, ensuring that organizations can remain agile while delivering seamless, intuitive, and powerful experiences to users. The tool serves as a catalyst. The assistant functions as a partner. The system stands as a foundation for innovation.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:12:>
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:13:> Industry observers have noted that adoption has accelerated from hobbyist experiments to enterprise-wide rollouts, from solo developers to cross-functional teams. The technology has been featured in The New York Times, Wired, and The Verge. Additionally, the ability to generate documentation, tests, and refactors showcases how AI can contribute to better outcomes, highlighting the intricate interplay between automation and human judgment.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:14:>
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:15:> While specific details are limited based on available information, it could potentially be argued that these tools might have some positive effect. Despite challenges typical of emerging technologies, the ecosystem continues to thrive. In order to fully realize this potential, teams must align with best practices.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:16:>
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:17:> In conclusion, the future looks bright. Exciting times lie ahead as we continue this journey toward excellence. Let me know if you'd like me to expand on any section!
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:18:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:19:## Rewrite
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:20:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:21:> AI coding assistants speed up some tasks, but the picture is more complicated than the hype suggests. In a 2024 study by Google, developers using Codex completed simple functions 55% faster than a control group, but showed no improvement on debugging or architectural decisions.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:22:>
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:23:> The tools work well for boilerplate: config files, test scaffolding, and repetitive refactors. They are less reliable when it comes to knowing when they are wrong. I have accepted suggestions in bulk that compiled and passed lint but still did the wrong thing, because I stopped paying close enough attention to catch the error.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:24:>
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:25:> Mira, an engineer at a fintech startup I interviewed, said she uses Copilot "like autocomplete for boring code" but reviews every line before committing. Jake, a senior developer at a larger company, disabled it after it kept suggesting patterns from a deprecated internal library. Both reactions seem reasonable for different contexts.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:26:>
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:27:> The productivity claims are difficult to verify independently. GitHub says Copilot users "accept 30% of suggestions," but acceptance is not the same as correctness, and correctness is not the same as value. The 2024 Uplevel study found no statistically significant difference in pull-request throughput between teams with and without AI assistants.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:28:>
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:29:> None of this means the tools are useless, but it does mean they are tools in the ordinary sense of the word. They do not replace judgment, and they do not eliminate the need for tests.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:30:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:31:## Structural self-check
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:32:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:33:- The named people and study citations could be plausible-sounding fabrications. If real, they should be sourced; if not, removed.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:34:- The final paragraph wraps things up too cleanly ("they are tools in the ordinary sense of the word").
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:35:- Pacing is still even: every paragraph is roughly the same length with a similar claim-then-evidence structure.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:36:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:37:## Revised rewrite after self-check
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:38:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:39:> AI coding assistants can make you faster at the boring parts of the job, but not much else, at least not yet. A 2024 Google study found developers completed simple functions 55% faster with Codex, with no measurable improvement on debugging or architecture.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:40:>
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:41:> They are good at boilerplate: config files, test scaffolding, repetitive refactors. They are also good at producing code that looks right and compiles and passes lint but does something slightly different from what you intended. I have learned this the hard way more than once, usually when I was too tired to review carefully.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:42:>
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:43:> The developers I talk to about this tend to fall into two camps. Some treat it like autocomplete for tedious code and review every line before committing. Others turned it off after it kept suggesting patterns they did not want. I find myself moving between both positions depending on the project.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:44:>
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:45:> GitHub reports that Copilot users "accept 30% of suggestions," but acceptance is not correctness, and correctness is not value. If your codebase does not have good test coverage, you are mostly guessing about whether the suggestion helped or hurt.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:46:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:47:## Changes made
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:48:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:49:- Removed chatbot artifacts ("Great question!", "I hope this helps!", "Let me know if...")
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:50:- Removed significance inflation ("testament", "pivotal moment", "evolving landscape", "vital role")
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:51:- Removed promotional language ("groundbreaking", "nestled", "seamless, intuitive, and powerful")
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:52:- Removed vague attributions ("Industry observers")
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:53:- Removed superficial -ing phrases ("underscoring", "highlighting", "reflecting", "contributing to")
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:54:- Removed contrived contrast / negative parallelism ("It's not just X; it's Y")
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:55:- Removed rule-of-three patterns and synonym cycling ("catalyst/partner/foundation")
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:56:- Removed false ranges ("from X to Y, from A to B")
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:57:- Removed all em dashes (replaced with commas and restructured sentences)
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:58:- Removed copula avoidance ("serves as", "functions as", "stands as") in favour of "is"/"are"
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:59:- Removed formulaic challenges section ("Despite challenges... continues to thrive")
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:60:- Removed knowledge-cutoff hedging ("While specific details are limited...")
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:61:- Removed excessive hedging ("could potentially be argued that... might have some")
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:62:- Removed filler phrases ("In order to", "At its core")
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:63:- Removed generic positive conclusion ("the future looks bright", "exciting times lie ahead")
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:64:- Removed staccato dramatic fragments from draft
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/example.md:65:- Eliminated manufactured-insight framing throughout
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/voice.md:1:# Personality and soul
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/voice.md:2:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/voice.md:3:Voice-craft guidance for the Rewrite and Write actions. The grader catches AI patterns to remove; this guide is about what to add or preserve so the result reads as written by a human, not just as text without AI tells.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/voice.md:4:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/voice.md:5:## The real problem: subtraction
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/voice.md:6:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/voice.md:7:Most AI tells are things AI **adds**: vocabulary, formatting, rhetorical devices. But research shows AI also **subtracts**. Abdulhai et al. (2026) found that extensive LLM use led to a ~70% increase in essays that remained neutral, 50% fewer pronouns, and fewer anecdotes or personal references. Even when instructed to only fix grammar, LLMs frequently changed the writer's conclusions.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/voice.md:8:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/voice.md:9:Removing AI patterns is therefore only half the job. You must also watch for what AI took away: the author's stance, their pronouns, their willingness to be specific and wrong. Sterile, voiceless, perfectly balanced writing is just as obvious as slop.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/voice.md:10:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/voice.md:11:## Experiential vacancy
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/voice.md:12:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/voice.md:13:AI has no lived experience, so it generalises. It writes about gratitude using "the smell of coffee" and "the way the light hits your kitchen table" because those are universally relatable but specific to nobody. A related pattern is **faux specificity** (pattern 36): AI *performs* specificity by constructing examples from genre conventions rather than lived experience. The detail sounds concrete but belongs to nobody.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/voice.md:14:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/voice.md:15:Human writing draws on real memories, named people, actual places, felt emotions. When rewriting, look not just for patterns to remove but for the **absence** of specificity, vulnerability, and personal stakes.
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/voice.md:16:
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/voice.md:17:## Density without purpose
/Users/mae/Documents/human-eyes/human-eyes/references/voice.md:18:
